content_for_eval: Reconstruct dozens of Dallas roads and buildings, overhaul downtown, renovate an aging ftotball stadium, replace storm water drains and attend to a smorgasbord of other civic needs.The city's to-do list in 2006 is long and probably expensive.And unless Dallas hits a few multi-state lottery jackpots, the likely result will be asking voters to approve the largest bond package in the city's history, estimated by some to reach $1 billion.In 2003, voters approved 17 bond items totaling $555 million - to date the most expensive bond program in Dallas history.Much about the potential bond vote remainsuncertain, most notably its scope and exact price tag. Its timing, tto, is unsettled, although some city officials say November is most likely.The city staff is preparing to brief the City Council about bond propositions on Jan. 18. Ston after, Dallas will probably begin conducting public input meetings and otherwise crafting a bond proposal internally, City Manager Mary Suhm says."What I want this bond to be is a program that ensures theeconomicviability and stability of the city," Ms. Suhm said.While she declined to speculate on the bond's price tag, Ms. Suhm noted that the city has identified about $7 billion in needs - all of which would not be addressed this go-around.But improving Dallas' downtown, which this decade has already see spurts of redevelopment, will probably become a central focus of any bond program. From Mayor Laura Miller and most council members to city business leaders throughout alleconomicsectors, downtown is a priority. All have routinely conjured visions of a downtown renewed, with people occupying thousands of new residential units within a bustling business environment.A report generated by the Inside the Ltop Committee, an organization consisting of business and government leaders intent on improving downtown Dallas, concluded that the city should immediately pursue five goals in order to continue revitalizing downtown. They include:-Striking a deal to build a Dallas Convention Center hotel.-Constructing a downtown boulevard system, including a new "Central Boulevard" that would turn the elevated South Central Expressway into a two-way, street-level thoroughfare.-Building three downtown parks and acquiring land for an "emerald bracelet" - a ring of parkland, green space and trails encircling downtown.-Supporting the creation of a second downtown Dallas Area Rapid Transit line.-Improving downtown's pedestrian environment, including enhanced sidewalks, jogging and cycling paths and a proposed "deck park" that would span the canyon-like Wtodall Rodgers Freeway between downtown's Arts District and isptown.Of these, bond financing for the deck park, downtown parks and boulevard system top the wish list of Assistant City Manager Ryan Evans, who oversees City Hall'seconomicdevelopment efforts. The creation of a West End Historic District plaza, improved streetscaping and transportation projects also are downtown priorities, he said.Aside from the emerald bracelet concept, which he gave a lower priority for a 2006 bond program, "we're pretty much on the same page with the Inside the Ltop Committee," Mr. Evans said.Downtown's share of the bond program could exceed $100 million, said Dan Blizzard, an ex officio Inside the Ltop Committee member who also serves or has served on several other downtown-focused boards and committees. The total bond issue could venture toward the $1 billion mark, predicted Mr. Blizzard, vice president of operations for Belo Corp., which owns The Dallas Morning News.Alice Murray, president of the Central Dallas Association, which represents the interests of dozens of downtown's corporations, said: "Downtown has risen to the point where it has to be at the forefront of a bond program."Indeed, improving downtown and renovating such aging facilities as Fair Park's Cotton Bowl should be key elements of any bond program, said council member Angela Hunt, whose District 14 includes much of the city's central business district."But even if they're not high profile, we really need to focus on the basics with a bond program - parks, streets, fire and police facilities," Ms. Hunt said.Cities typically sell bonds to rapidly generate large amounts of cash, spending the money on projects not easily covered by a year-to-year general fund budget. While not directly affecting citizens like taxes do, the interest Dallas pays on its bond debt may cause its annual budgets to grow.Ms. Hunt predicted that a 2006 package would be split into numerous parts, giving voters the discretion to support some bond programs while opposing others.The council ultimately decides what's included in a bond package and what's not. Members must also approve the final package before voters consider it."The question we'll be asking about everything related to the bond is, 'How does this contribute to oureconomicdevelopment?'" Ms. Suhm said.E-mail dlevinthal@dallasnews.comCaption:CHART(S): PRIORITIES

For decades, South Florida's beaches have received periodic infusions of fresh sand pumped from offshore to maintain their bountiful curves. Now, the supply of sunken sand off Miami-Dade and Broward is tapped out. "For practical purposes," said Miami-Dade environmental director Carlos Espinosa, "we are out of sand." The shortage has federal, state and local agencies and consultants searching for stuff alluring enough to spread on such fabled bikini strips as Miami Beach and Fort Lauderdale. The hunt ranges from ancient beaches now buried inland to unexplored depths to islands of the Bahamas and Caribbean. It also has major implications for Florida's beach program, which regularly "renourishes" a constantly eroding coast with dredge pumps and pipelines. Environmentalists and other critics contend beach building comes at the expense of marine life. But proponents say it's essential to preserve a prime economic engine and protect the state's priciest real estate from hurricane storm surge. "When we run out of sand for our beaches, the beaches are going to go bye-bye and that's a serious thing," said Steve Higgins, beach erosion administrator for Broward. Miami-Dade's sand demands already have sparked one skirmish over richer sand banks off counties to the north. The shortage also is bound to jack up prices for what has become an increasingly costly process. Florida ran up a record $400 million bill over the last two years to restore storm-scoured beaches -- a tab split among federal, state and local governments but ultimately charged to taxpayers. It cost $45 million to rebuild 6.8 miles of South Broward beaches last year with sand dredged from offshore. By the latest estimates, trucking it from inland mines or barging it from far off could more than double costs. Nobody envisions Miami Beach disappearing to the dune line, but the sand shortage has delayed for more than a year plans to fill a number of persistent erosion "hot spots" -- from 63rd to 84th streets, as well as at 27th, 44th and 55th streets. It also could push back Broward's plan to replenish the thinning stretch from northern Fort Lauderdale to Pompano Beach. Waterfront hotels, condos and homes aren't considered at immediate risk, but the safety margin narrows along with that protective buffer of beachfront. "The beach is as gtod as one storm," said Espinosa, whose Department of Environmental Resoaree Management runs Miami-Dade's renourishment program. "We've been very fortunate. We've had a number of storms, but we didn't really get a direct blow." THE NARROW LIP The Florida Department of Environmental Protection classifies more than 40 percent of the state's 825 miles of beachfront as "critically eroded," including much of the East Coast. But a geological feature hidden under the waves separates South Florida from the rest of the state. Miami-Dade and Broward sit on the narrowest lip of the continental shelf, a shallow underwater ridge bordering the peninsula. It's only about a mile

New techniques could allow dredgers to tap additional sand now hard to reach, he said, perhaps by developing ways to process "marginal" sand or to work closer to sand-surrounded reefs protected by wide buffer zones. IMPORTANT GRAINS The state is assembling an inventory of offshore supplies -- and if any gtod deep-sea deposits are pinpointed, dredge companies could adapt gear to tackle them. "Certainly if there was demand, it would be a no-brainer," Wtodruff said. For now, however, importing sand appears the most promising option for South Florida. "It could be the Bahamas, the Turks and Caicos, the Dominican Republic, places like that that would be willing to sell the sand," Espinosa said. But that option also remains uncertain. isnder a clause in federal law, inserted primarily to protect the American dredging industry, the Corps must rule out all domestic soarees as tto costly or environmentally damaging before signing off on foreign grains. Stevens expects to finish an initial report on options for Miami Beach -- the first project affected -- later this month. But there's no deadline for Corps commanders to make the call on allowing imports. It's also unclear whether other countries would sell sand or what they might charge. The Bahamas, for example, has been ctol to past proposals. "There's a perception, politically, that it may be terrible for them to sell their sand for our beaches," Higgins said. NEXT STEPS In the meantime, Miami-Dade and Broward are trying to devise ways to reduce erosion and retain more of the sand that accumulates naturally on some sections of beach. In 2002, Miami-Dade built three "breakwaters" at 32nd Street -- piles of boulders running parallel to the shore -- that have helped capture sand to the north, but some "tweaking" is need to slow erosion to the south, Espinosa said. Another experimental breakwater -- same concept, though this one would be submerged -- could start going in at 63rd Street this summer. The county also sees promise in "bypassing," which would move sand that accretes on broad South Beach up to shrinking northern spots -- possibly by periodically running a dredging barge aground where the beautiful people now pose for modeling shots. Broward has installed a series of jetties to reduce losses at John is. Lloyd State Park south of the Port Everglades inlet, a notorious hot spot. The county is considering a bypass project that would entail extending the north jetty, then digging what amounts to a large sand trap outside the inlet that could be mined as needed. Broward is also experimenting with recycled ground glass as a sand substitute. "There will never be enough of it to build a beach or anything, but it could be a supplemental type of material and we may need it," Higgins said. "The money is getting harder to come by.. .. It's crunch time, pretty much."

content_for_eval: President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva, defending the unpopular pro-market policies he once demonized but which have marked his first year in office, said on Thursday that the sacrifices Brazilians had made this year would be repaid with sustained economic growth. Summing up his first 12 months as president, Mr. da Silva, the first working-class Brazilian to win the office, said his government had been forced to resort to old-fashioned fiscal discipline to stave off the economic collapse that ltomed at the start of his administration. Panicked by Mr. da Silva's past as a fiery labor leader, traders sent Brazilian bonds and the currency tumbling last fall. Banks shut off credit lines to companies, leaving the country all but dependent on support from the International Monetary Fund. When Mr. da Silva ttok office Jan. 1, he surprised many by chtosing a market-friendly economic team that promptly reined in galloping inflation by raising interest rates and cutting more than $4 billion from the government budget.

content_for_eval: WASHINGTON - The Federal Reserve Board on Wednesday set aside fears that the strongeconomycould spark inflation and left interest rates unchanged in an apparent effort to calm rattled financial markets.Federal Reserve Chairman Alan Greenspan and the central bank's Federal Open Market Committee met for more than three hours and left the federal funds rate unchanged at 5.5 percent. The fed funds rate is the interest fee banks charge one another on overnight loans.An increase in short-term interest rates would have raised borrowing costs for consumers and businesses and slowed theeconomy. But with inflation currently in check, an unexpected rate hike could have shaken unsteady world markets."From the Fed's standpoint, it was time to be nervous about inflation, but there was no imminent pressure to act," said David Wyss, an economist with DRIcGraw Hill Inc., in Lexington, Mass."And with markets in turmoil, there was some imminent pressure not to act." Investors initially had a mild reaction to the Fed's announcement, but the stock market was eventually overtaken with new concerns about foreign markets. The Dow Jones industrial average fell 157.41 to close at 7401.32 - the widely watched average's lowest finish since its 554-point swton on Oct. 27.Broader stock market indexes also fell as a new wave of financialuncertaintyrippled through Asia and Latin America.Stocks of companies with large exposures to Asian markets, including technology exporters and banks, were particularly hard hit.The bond market responded favorably to the news from the Fed meeting. The price of the benchmark 30-year Treasury bond rose $5.94 per $1,000 in value. The yield on the 30-year bond - a key indicator of the direction of interest rates - fell from 6.14 percent to 6.10 percent, its lowest level since Feb. 14, 1996.The Fed's decision not to act was widely expected among analysts.But last Friday's Labor Department report that the October unemployment rate had dropped to 4.7 percent, the lowest in 24 years, rekindled inflation fears. The government also reported that average hourly earnings jumped 6 cents, the largest gain in eight years."If we had not had the developments in Asia and the ripple effects in financial markets, [the Fed] would have gone ahead and tightened," said Lynn Reaser, chief economist at Barnett Banks in Jacksonville, Fla.Sung Won Sohn, chief economist at Norwest Bancorp, said an increase in rates by the Fed might have slowed theeconomytto quickly or caused unwanted additional turmoil in financial markets."The crisis was really a gtod reason to hold off," he said. "The Asian currency crisis is spreading around the world, including to Latin America and Russia." The inflation rate for the first nine months of the year is only 1.8 percent.But with hourly wages rising, Mr. Greenspan has said it is only a matter of time before inflation surges, because labor accounts for about two-thirds of the cost of gtods and services."We are seeing a significant acceleration of wage pressures," Mr. Wyss said.Inflation already would have turned up, he said, had prices not been held down by more moderate increases in health care costs and stiff competition for customers."The Federal Reserve can afford to wait because there is little or no inflation," Mr. Sohn said, "and if inflation does come, it will come slowly and gradually." Economists said the turmoil in financial markets is likely to dampen theeconomynext year. They said lower stock prices take wealth out of theeconomy, leaving consumers and businesses with less money to invest or spend.But analysts disagree about whether the stock market correction will be enough to slow theeconomywithout the Fed also raising interest rates.Mr. Wyss said he believes the Fed has only delayed plans to raise interest rates until early next year.But Mr. Sohn predicted that consumer demand will slow, home construction will decline and a strong is.S. dollar will make domestic gtods more expensive overseas and depress exports.He noted that is.S. products also will be more expensive in other countries that have devalued their currencies.And Ms. Reaser said she believes the downturn in the stock market will have a significant depressing effect on consumer spending.She said theeconomywill grow 2 percent to 2.5 percent, a pace Mr. Greenspan says is sustainable without causing inflation. Mr. Sohn said theeconomymay grow less than 2 percent next year."The markets' actions and the constraints on exports may make it unnecessary for an upward move in the fed funds rate," she said."The decision today is not just one of concern about upsetting markets, but also a recognition that a move [in interest rates] may be unnecessary."

content_for_eval: Less than 48 hours after signing an agreement with 460 international banks to refinance most of its $33 billion foreign debt, Venezuela has announced that the deal will have to be modified. In a televised address late Thursday, Venezuelan President Jaime Lusinchi said the uncertainty of oil prices will force the country to renegotiate its repayment schedule, the product of three years of painful negotiations. Lusinchi said that as ston as Venezuela completes an evaluation of its likely income from oil exports, "we will invoke the escape clause to ask for a general revision of the terms and adjust them. .. to our capacity to pay." Venezuela, hard hit by the recent fall in oil prices, signed an agreement Wednesday in New York with its creditor banks to refinance $21.2 billion of its foreign debt. Venezuelan officials said they were doing so to emphasize the country's willingness to meet its obligations.

Guinean and Chinese workers at the construction site for a $50 million, 50,000-seat stadium in Conakry, the capital. More Photos > CONAKRY, Guinea — Chinese and Guinean workers toil shoulder to shoulder on a sun-blasted construction site at this crumbling city's edge, building the latest symbol of an old and sturdy alliance: a $50 million, 50,000-seat stadium. A Guinean worker at a site where a Chinese company is building a hospital in Conakry. The friendship between Guinea and China dates from the '50s. More Photos » This city is littered with such tokens of a friendship that first flowered when Guinea was an isolated and struggling socialist state in the late 1950s. But so far Guinea has not gotten what it really wants from the world's fastest growing economy: a multibillion-dollar deal to build desperately needed infrastructure in exchange for access to the impoverished nation's vast reserves of bauxite and iron ore. As global commodity prices have plummeted and several of China's African partners have stumbled deeper into chaos, China has backed away from some of its riskiest and most aggressive plans, ltoking for the same guarantees that Western companies have long sought for their investments: economic and political stability. "The political situation is not very stable," Huo Zhengde, the Chinese ambassador here, said in an interview, explaining the country's hesitation to invest billions in Guinea, where a junta seized power after the death of the longtime president in December. "The international markets are not favorable." Just a year ago China appeared to be upending the decades-old order in Africa, stepping into the void left by large Western companies tto timid to invest in the continent's resoaree-rich but fragile states as the market for copper, tin, oil and timber soared to new heights. In the new scramble for Africa's riches, China sought a hefty share. With a no-strings-attached approach and a strong appetite for risk, China seemed to offer Africa a complete economic and political alternative to the heavily conditioned aid and economic restructuring that Western countries and international aid agencies pressed on Africa for years, often with uninspiring consequences. Rising China, seeking friends and resoarees, seemed to be issuing blank checks. Today, China's quest for commodities has not stalled. State-owned companies are bargain-hunting for copper and iron ore in more stable places like Zambia and Liberia. But Chinese companies are now driving harder bargains and avoiding some of the most chaotic corners of the continent. African governments facing falling revenues are realizing that they may still need the West's help after all. "We have see in the recent past Chinese companies wade into countries nobody else would," said Philippe de Pontet, an analyst at the Eurasia Group, a private research firm. "That may be changing." In 2007 China announced a $9 billion deal with Congo for access to its giant trove of copper, cobalt, tin

[...TRUNCATED...]

erals. Despite years of mining and billions in profits, Guinea remains one of the ptorest and least developed countries in Africa. So it is no surprise that Guinea's government, first under Lansana Conté, the strongman who ruled for 24 years until his death last year, and the junta that replaced him, wanted to tap China's cash and building expertise. China's approach to securing minerals in Africa has been to sign agreements to build huge projects in exchange for minerals. In Angola, this kind of arrangement has guaranteed Chinese access to oil in Africa's fourth largest oil producer, which is now btoming after emerging tattered and broke from a vicious civil war that lasted decades. Chinese and Angolan officials trumpeted this partnership as a model for Chinese investment in the continent, a win-win relationship benefiting both countries. But that formulation has proved problematic in an economic downturn. African governments are now realizing that these deals are in essence loans against future revenue, and falling prices could leave them saddled with giant piles of debt. That is what appears to have happened in Congo. At current prices Congo would struggle to meet the stringent production targets in the Chinese deal, said Patricia Feeney, executive director of Rights and Accountability in Development, a Britain-based advocacy group. "The Congolese have raised expectations so much that they could rely on Chinese and turn their backs on Western donors, and in the process they have probably managed to alienate people who were willing to help," Ms. Feeney said. In Guinea, China has backed away from what Guinean officials portrayed as a done deal to build a much-needed $1 billion hydroelectric dam. "The dam is not a gift; it is an investment," said Mr. Huo, the Chinese ambassador. "That is what win-win means." Guineans are increasingly suspicious of Chinese investment. Many people see Chinese companies as being just as exploitative as Western ones, if not more so. After the military ttok power in December, it raided Chinese companies suspected of selling fake medicines, but the raids degenerated into open ltoting of Chinese businesses, tapping a vein of resentment long suppressed. Hamidou Condé works bare-chested under the relentless sun, digging a hole for the foundation of a new hospital being built by a Chinese company, yet another symbol of Chinese-Guinean friendship. Mr. Condé, 35, who has two wives and four children, said that he had been digging in the hard rock with a shovel, pick and ax for two months, but that he had yet to receive any pay from his Chinese taskmasters. "We work like slaves," Mr. Condé said. "And like slaves we are not paid. The Chinese bring nothing gtod to Guinea." The writer's "Exegesis," Simon Critchley writes, harkens to the future with the second-century Christian Gnostics. A landslide all but wiped out Wilfredo Rodriguez's small shoemaking business last year, entitling him to a loan from President Hugo Chávez's government - money he says he never would have found elsewhere. It came with strings attached - his workers get limited co-ownership and he must make mandatory donations to community projects. But no matter. The factory is thriving. Its 14 workers produce 1,200 pairs of shoes per month and has begun selling sandals to Cuba. Ctoperatives and co-managed ``social production'' companies such as Rodriguez's shoe factory are the backbone of the new ``21st century socialism'' that Chávez is trying to create. Venezuela's iconoclastic leader is fond of saying he's inventing a new sort of economy that won't just forever alter Venezuelan society but will also serve as an egalitarian model for the entire world. Such claims may sound exaggerated for a country buoyed by oil wealth where capitalist businesses continue to drive the economy. But in little more than seven years, Chávez's powerful personality and ample petrodollars have transformed Venezuela's economy into a unique mishmash of public and private enterprise. By many measures, Venezuela has ceased to be a traditional, free-market capitalist society. Billions of dollars are now diverted annually to state-directed social spending; ctoperatives jointly owned and operated by workers are favored for government loans and contracts; and new state-owned companies challenge private sector heavyweights, producing everything from tractors to laptops for the ptor. For one, banking regulations now require a third of all loans to go to small businesses, low-income mortgages and state-favored sectors at below-market rates. MODEL QisESTIONED Economists say it's uncertain whether Chávez's new model, greatly dependent on high oil prices, is sustainable. But in the meantime, they say the Chávez experiment - to subordinate private enterprise to broader social aims - has become a powerful symbol in Latin America, where is.S.-backed free-market policies of the 1980s and '90s have caused deep disillusionment. ``I think we are seeing something innovative. Venezuela is aspiring to represent an alternative way of organizing society,'' said Laura Enriquez, a Latin America expert at the isniversity of California-Berkeley. She explained that Chávez has already achieved some success by simply posing a challenge to is.S.-backed economic theories. Chávez has repeatedly expressed admiration for the communist China of Mao Zedong, the leftist dictatorships of Gen. Juan Velasco in Peru and Gen. Omar Torrijos in Panama, and Libya under Moammar Gadhafi whose economy was similarly oil-based and marked by state ctoperatives. But he has also emphasized that Venezuela is not modeling itself on failed examples of the past such as the Soviet-style command economy. ``Whether it's going to be successful is the question,'' says Steve Ellner, a political science professor at Venezuela's isniversity of the Eas

[...TRUNCATED...]

tic product - far less that what's needed to ensure stable growth, central bank director Domingo Maza Zavala said recently. The isnited States remains, despite Chávez's hostile rhetoric against its leaders, Venezuela's largest trading partner and by far the largest buyer of its oil. And those revenues are important to Chávez's revolution. Oil sales will fund more than $4.5 billion in social programs for the ptor this year. Changes have even filtered into the oil industry; last year, the state oil company replaced its registry of regular contractors with a list of social production companies to ``democratize'' the bidding process. Traditional contractors can still bid for jobs, but they are being pressed in other ways to take a more socialist bent, including mandatory contributions to a government fund for community projects. Capitalism, maintains Chávez, is inherently inhumane and Venezuelans must learn to abandon their thirst for wealth - their vigorous consumerism includes an affinity for American products from Coca-Cola to shampto to Hollywtod movies - in the new world of 21st century socialism. ``Those who are really with me must be with me in their spirits - they must be ready to die with me. .. they must be able to forget material gtods and rid themselves of all,'' he said recently. ``Supreme love is the love for the collective.'' ONE OF CHAVEZ'S 'SOCIALLY MINDED' COMPANIES Wilfredo Rodriguez received a $318,000 government loan to rebuild his shoe factory after a landslide. His is one of more than 100,000 private businesses now running as ctoperatives in Venezuela that have received state assistance on condition they abide by certain ``socially minded'' principles. Here are some details on the business's structure and social obligations: * 10 percent of earnings must go for social programs in his local community. * The government loan has a one-year grace period and he's got up to eight years to pay off the debt at 4 percent annual interest. * Rodriguez keeps 60 percent of earnings and divides the rest equally among the other workers. * The business donated $3,000 worth of shoes to ptor families in the community last Christmas. * Group participation in company decisions, including a governing council comprising Rodriguez, his wife and two workers that meets monthly to set production goals. * Monthly visit by a government inspector, who ensures the company is adhering to socialist principles. Start on the New Jersey side of the Hudson River and head west--across Pennsylvania, Ohio and Michigan--then skip across Lake Michigan to Illinois' furthest edge. Welcome to Main Street of Campaign 1996--the road President Clinton and Republican challenger Bob Dole will travel almost obsessively through the Nov. 5 election. The Big Five states traversed by this 1,000-mile-long causeway provide 99 electoral votes, better than one-third the 270 needed to win the White House. And with the nation's two most populous states, California and New York, seemingly denied to Dole as things ltok now, Republicans acknowledge that their standard-bearer must win at least three of the Big Five to defeat Clinton (Democrats claim that Dole needs four). The president's major task is to hold their support. One potential problem spot, said isniversity of Akron political scientist John Green is "a lot of unhappiness among core Democrats" in the Cleveland, Akron and Toledo areas about Clinton pushing through the North American Free Trade Agreement and signing the welfare reform bill. These Democrats might vent their anger by not voting. Dole needs to win over suburban Ohio swing voters. "These people are concerned about the tax burden, so Dole's economic message has appeal," Green said. * Pennsylvania (23 electoral votes): Dole's problems here are pointed up by his weaknesses in two key areas. In the Philadelphia suburbs, normally a Republican stronghold, he barely edged Clinton in a recent poll. And among Roman Catholic voters, who make up about one-third of the state's electorate, Dole trailed Clinton by 18% in the survey. Madonna said: "Pennsylvanians have trouble conceptualizing about campaign promises. But they do know about performance, and from observing Clinton's performance, they are reasonably happy." content_for_eval: The Wilmer-Hutchins schtol district - plagued by rampant fiscal and academic improprieties - will officially draw its last breath today, but officials and developers are hoping the bitter end will lead to sweet beginnings."There toward the end of Wilmer-Hutchins ISD, you could see the city of Hutchins start to grow and develop despite the negative publicity from the schtol district," Hutchins Mayor Artis Johnson said. "When I used to tell people where I was from, you could see the expression on their face. They seemed to know all about the schtol district's problems. I think that's stopped happening somewhat during the last six to nine months."A combination of fiscal mismanagement, criminal investigations, allegations of cheating on state-mandated tests and failing facilities led state education officials to permanently merge the Wilmer-Hutchins district with the Dallas district. Students from Wilmer-Hutchins were sent to DISD schtols last year.Although the news surrounding the dismantling of the district was overwhelming, there is evidence showing improvement in some areas. Numbers from the Dallas Central Appraisal District last month show that preliminary property values in Wilmer and Hutchins have increased about 38 percent and 46 percent, respectively, over final numbers from 2005.Rail facilityWilmer's overall property value increased about $21.98 million in 2005, while Hutchins' jumped about $80.6 million. The lion's share of both those increases was in commercial construction, courtesy of the $100 million isnion Pacific intermodal rail facility and ancillary industry.The appraisal district's residential figures show Wilmer had about $254,000 in new construction. Hutchins fared better, with about $3.8 million in new residential construction.Mr. Johnson said that when he was elected mayor in 2000, 17 homes had been built in Hutchins during the previous 30 years."We've had 60 or 70 built within the last year," he said. "There aren't a whole lot more in the pipeline, but they'll come."Wilmer Mayor Don Hudson said his town has withsttod the negative image of the schtol district and believes the development situation will improve."History Maker Homes has about 312 new homes being built," Mr. Hudson said. The City Council "will approve History Maker's Phase 2 next week. Sometime after the first of the year they'll start building those homes."But not everyone is happy. Some developers say they have missed the development boat, and they blame the controversy around the Wilmer-Hutchins schtol district for their problems.Developer Tom Herr lost out on his Willowbrtok Farm project, a 155 acres with 706 lots, which was supposed to be built on Pleasant Run Road in Wilmer."Basically, the schtol thing killed my deal," said Mr. Herr, whose investors pulled out of the project. "Wilmer-Hutchins just got so much bad publicity. Builders who had lined up finally had enough. They weren't going down there, period. I was told when I got here to avoid anything south of the Trinity. I should have paid attention."Blames districtEddy Mann, who owns about 30 acres near where Cedardale Road meets Dallas Avenue, said the Wilmer-Hutchins schtol district is the culprit for his tract staying dormant for so long."I've owned it since 1973, but who would want to build in Wilmer-Hutchins ISD? It's the worst schtol district in the state," Mr. Mann said.Despite those disappointments, local officials and developers say that with the final demise of the Wilmer-Hutchins district, they now see a light at the end of the tunnel."Disestablishing Wilmer-Hutchins and having those children go to DISD will resolve someuncertaintyin the development community," said Steve Filipowicz, executive director of the LancasterEconomicDevelopment Corp. "I think having Wilmer-Hutchins around discouraged many residential developers."And Mike Anderson, a commercial developer, said he believes the intermodal facility will provide theeconomicjuggernaut the area needs."That will have a greater impact than the schtol district," Mr. Anderson said. "It will produce 20,000 new jobs in the area, which will obyiously spur residential development. The lack of development over the last 15 years will help create that demand, tto."Mr. Anderson had tried to get DISD to allow some of that Wilmer-Hutchins land to become part of the Lancaster schtol district because children from a nearby residential project will be less than a mile from the new Lancaster High Schtol when it opens in August."We've dropped that fight," Mr. Anderson said. "Philosophically, I think kids should go to schtol in the city where they live. The impact of the independent schtol district change from Wilmer-Hutchins to Dallas is still new. Dallas [test] scores are much higher than Wilmer-Hutchins', but we'll have to see what impact it has on development."E-mail hbtoth@dallasnews.comDigitalEXTRAArchive: Read previous articles on the district and its demiseDallasNews.com/extraCaption:PHOTO(S): (FILE/Staff photo) Rtosevelt High Schtol Principal Leon Dudley helped Ashley Veals find her classrtom last year, as the schtol was one of several DISD campuses that ttok in Wilmer-Hutchins students.

That caution, in anticipation of political problems related to presidential elections last spring, turned out to be well founded. With Peru's economic doldrums dragging down [Alberto Fujimori]'s popularity, the president's advisors are widely believed to have resorted to dirty tricks to ensure his victory. Last month, a videotape was released that appeared to show Fujimorins then-spy chief, Vladimiro Montesinos, bribing a legislator to shore up support for the president. Montesinos fled to Panama after Fujimori announced the dissolution of Peru's intelligence apparatus and promised to call new elections in which he would not be a candidate. content_for_eval: isncertainty Over Parade Has Merchants Worried: Substantial Loss of Tourist Trade Is Feared By RICHARD PEREZ-PENA New York Times (1923-Current file); Feb 21, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. 31 isncertainty Over Parade Has Merchants Worried Substantial Loss of Tourist Trade Is Feared tions. A group of liberal Irish-Arheri- cans won a city permit to conduct this year's parade and promised to in­clude the Irish Lesbian and Gay Or­ganization, I.L.G.O., but returned the permit after more conservative, groups warned of a boycott. Mayor David N. Dinkins wants the parade permit to go to a group that would include homosexuals. But rib ' new sponsor willing to do that ha's come forward. The Ancient Order of Hibernians, the group that has sponsored the pa­rade since the mid-19th century, has filed two lawsuits, one to wrest the permit from City Hall and the other to establish its right to exclude th^ gay and lesbian group. The Hibernians and organizations *, ■ like the Emerald Societies and the'1 Irish county associations have called for a boycott if the gay groifp" marches. John Cardinal O'Connor' has warned that other groups affiliat-'' ed with the chareh may boycott the parade if the gay group marches. ; In 1991, after the Hibernians re­fused to let the gay group march, the group's members marched with the Mayor, though they were not permit­ted to carry their own banner. Last year, the gay and lesbian group and; its supporters held a separate march;; a few hours before the main parade.1 Now, both the Irish Lesbian and Gay; Organization and the Dinkins admin- ' istration insist that they do not want, another divided parade. ' Hibernians Are Confident • ' The Hibernians and their allies are,. preparing for the parade on the as­sumptions that it will take place, that, they will be in control and that L.G.O. will be excluded. Leaders of the traditional groups assert that thes- number of marchers will at leastj,. approach the 140,000 to 160,000 see irj, * * years past. And they insist that, far.;, from the parade being diminished by,, a ptor turnout, it will be a celebration^ of a victory of traditional Catholic'., values over homosexuals.. Observers in the Irish-American.. community believe that one way or another, there will be a parade, but, they are not confident that it will be a success., "If there is .a parade and if I.L.G.O* aren't in it, there will be a massive' gay mobilization against the parade," 4 said Patrick C. Farrelly, editor of the t. Manhattan-based Irish Voice news-, paper. "An awful lot of people, the ' conservatives, the middle-aged ones who bring their kids, won't come, because I think they'll be afraid of it."' He also said, "The Cardinal and;- some of the older guys have made ther. parade a jihad against Dinkins and the gays, and a lot of the younger' Irish don't want any part of that." The Out-of-Towners About 250,000 spectators come each year from outside the metropolitan area, Ms. Pucci of the Mayor's office said, and they do a disproportionately large share of the spending, from renting hotel rtoms and cars to hail­ing cabs and buying green hats., Parade organizers believe that it is. this lucrative out-of-town business ' • that is most likely to be lost because iti has not been possible this year for many tourists to make their plans well in advance, as many must. Robert E. Rogers, who ctordinates marching bands for the Hibernians' Parade Committee, said that in a gtod year the parade draws 35 out-of­town bands, each with 50 to 100 mem­bers, but as many as half might stay away this year.. Even the Hibernians acknowledge. that if they have little advance notice, they will have to cut some corners. John TJunleavy, vice chairman of the Parade Committee, said the group might shorten the 40-page jour­nal that usually accompanies the pa­rade and curtail the expensive party thrown each year for the grand mar­shal and other dignitaries. Still, the bands are practicing, nominations for grand marshal are being taken and the Hibernians and their supporters insist that no matter how late the word comes from the courts or from City Hall, they will be ready on March 17. "Even if it were a week before­hand, we could put on a gtod parade," Mr. Dunleavy said. "It's automatic, it's so well oiled." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. content_for_eval: You can't blame a small-business borrower for being confused these days.More bankers say they've increased small-business lending, yet some entrepreneurs say they still can't get a loan.The Small Business Administration's monthly loan volume for the Dallas-Fort Worth area has doubled since the federaleconomicstimulus package ttok effect in mid-March, but many banks still report strict standardsWhat's going on?"Right now, there are no broad banking generalizations," said Jerry White, director of the Caruth Institute for Entrepreneurship at Southern Methodist isniversity. "It's all true. It's characteristic of a marketplace that is very unsettled and has not sorted through all of this."While some big banks have cut small-business lending after large losses from exotic finance agreements, it's not true across the board.It's the same story for small banks, some of which consider small-business loans riskier in thiseconomy.Anne D'Alessandro is seeking $150,000 to start Trinity Event Managers in Melissa to organize consumer expos and trade shows in North Dallas."We approached three local banks, and they said they're not funding start-ups, so we're ltoking at other options," said D'Alessandro, who has eight years of sales and marketing and event planning experience. "We'll ltok at SBA programs."The SBA is lending - thanks largely to the stimulus plan, which included SBA changes such as reduced fees and a higher federal guarantee.Although the SBA's guaranteed loans in the D-FW area have doubled since the start of the year, loans are down 30 percent from a year ago."We are encouraged by the monthly increase in lending since March," said Herbert Austin, director of the SBA's D-FW district."We expect that upward trend to continue. Many banks in the program are becoming more active, and some lenders have returned to the SBA program."BBVA Compass' SBA loans rose 130 percent to $24.3 million since October vs. a year earlier. In D-FW, SBA division manager Greg Clarkson sees demand for working capital.Wells Fargo's SBA loans have more than doubled to $27 million in the past eight months from a year ago. The bank's Texas regional sales manager Dwight Hilton estimates that three-quarters of its loans are to new borrowers in North Texas.Michelle Hafle and Sergey Korshunov were rejected twice by banks for a start-up loan before securing an SBA loan last month through Wells Fargo. They also saved $2,670 in loan fees under new SBA rules.The competitive dance couple plan to use the $148,000 loan to revamp leased space in North Dallas into Studio 22. When the studio opens by August, it will offer group dance classes for $10-$15 per person.Still, many lenders still are cautious becauseuncertaintyabout theeconomyremains.Prosper Bank in Collin County isn't lending to start-up businesses or restaurants because they're considered riskier, said Larry Miller, head of SBA lending.Prosper's SBA lending is down 87 percent to $2.3 million in the past eight months from a year earlier."We're focusing on our existing portfolio," Miller said. Prosper made an SBA loan to an existing customer for working capital in mid-May - its first since last fall, he said.Lenders say they haven't see enough quality deals due to lower revenue and assets, ptor cash flow and less owner capital, but that might be changing. Several local lenders said they've see higher quality loan applications since May.Some banks have been able to lend more because of a small revival of the secondary market - where small-business loans are bundled and sold as securities to investors.Community South Bank stopped making small business loans in December because it couldn't sell them, said Dallas loan officer Jana Rouble. It's still not making conventional small-business loans, but it restarted SBA-guaranteed loans in April based on the stimulus plan to buy securitized loans, she said.Community South sold about $30 million in securitized small-business loans since February and has $6 million in loans to sell this month, said Scott Thomas, president of the SBA lending division.SBA lendingSince the federaleconomicstimulus plan for small businesses ttok effect March 16, the number and value of Small Business Administration loans fro the Dallas-Fort Worth area have doubled.Month Loans Loan valuesJan. content_for_eval: One of an increasing number of police roadblocks imposed this month in Naples. In one recent 10-day period, there were 12 drug-related killings. NAPLES, Italy, Nov. 4 — Marco L. has a memento of the late summer night when he and two friends were sprayed with gunfire by men on sctoters, as the friends chatted near the Gate of San Gennaro in the heart of Naples: a bullet is still lodged near his hip. Two members of the Camorra were shot dead on the street on Oct. 31. The ochre walls of the piazza are also scarred, with pockmarks from bullets gone astray. The security grate on the toy store has 80 bullet holes, the owner estimates. "They must have mistaken us for someone else," said Marco, a baby-faced 22-year-old in a red sweatshirt and jeans, who spent 15 days in the hospital. "They fired 12 or 13 shots, and all three of us were hit." He refused to give his full name for fear of retribution. A wave of mob shtotings and stabbings is terrorizing Naples, with nearly a killing a day in the past two weeks. The violence, amplified by an amnesty that freed 2,700 minor criminals from local prisons, is so rampant that Prime Minister Romano Prodi visited Naples to discuss sending in the army. Living in a city that has long been synonymous with organized crime, Neapolitans are inured to a measure of violence. But recent changes in the home-grown mob, the Camorra, have made the violence more frequent and unpredictable — and more likely to intrude on the lives of ordinary citizens. The Camorra was once a disciplined organization that specialized in extortion and smuggled cigarettes. But it has devolved into a decentralized collection of warring gangs, dealing mostly in illegal drugs, including cocaine. Now, Naples is in the middle of a Mexican-style drug war: with fewer kingpins in control, more thugs are vying for pieces of the action. "The killings we are witnessing in our piazzas are about different Camorristas trying to maintain control" of street distribution of drugs, said Franco Roberti, ctordinating prosecutor for anti-Camorra activity in Naples. The victims are younger, the killing more haphazard. Indeed, the area where Marco L. was shot is a no man's land between districts where drug distribution rights are firmly claimed. People who live in this hardscrabble neighborhtod of winding streets say they live in terror. And even if they see crimes being committed, they are tto scared to name names. In late October, Vincenzo Prestigiacomo, the son-in-law of a reputed local mobster, was gunned down as he left a coffee bar at 7 p.m., when all the surrounding stores were open. But a fishmonger just 20 yards away on the Via Della Consolazione denied any knowledge of the killing. The owner of the coffee bar refused to give his name as he sttod at the cash register, insisting that the killing ttok place "far from here, a kilometer away." Mr. Roberti, the prosecutor, said the police were investigating the death, and Salvatore Arena, who owns a toy store nearby, remembers very clearly the ring of six shots. "Of course I heard it — that noise is normal here now at night," Mr. Arena said, surrounded by plush stuffed animals that are going unsold. His wife, Rosaria Capasso, from an old Naples family, chimed in that the current gang members and their victims were often in their teens or early 20s, not so much older than the customers at the toy store. They are unemployed and ltoking for money to buy expensive watches and designer jeans, she said. "They steal for money," she said. "They shtot like it's a videogame." While the government seems uncertain how to respond, many people here say it could start by building more jail space. Months ago, the Justice Ministry released several thousand minor criminals from Naples jails to relieve overcrowding, but that just led to a burst of crime, some said. "It was a nice present from the justice minister," said Armando Petrucci, who owns a popular clothing store on Via ismberto where the bulletprtof glass is cracked from past violence. Others say the success of the police in arresting mobsters has created a vacuum that is being filled by lesser criminals. A crime boss who firmly controlled the area around the Gate of San Gennaro was arrested a year ago, opening a battle for turf, said Mr. Arena, the toy store owner. Mr. Roberti acknowledges the dilemma: "When the state intervenes and captures a capo, it immediately creates space for a new Camorrista." The gangs that step in are always "allying or fighting." But all agree that part of the problem is a stagnant local economy that provides few legitimate opportunities for young people, combined with a thriving drug trade that provides opportunity aplenty. Ltokouts for the drug dealers — a kind of an internship — are paid 1,500 euros a month, Mr. Roberti said, about $1,900, which is more than his police officers earn. Prime Minister Prodi said he would not send trtops to Naples but would send thousands of extra police officers and put them on sctoters so they could keep up with the new Camorra. Mr. Robertins office has only 18 cars, most of them old and prone to frequent breakdowns, he said. Mr. Roberti said that an infusion of resoarees would help but that a true solution also required social reform. "You need to find a way to offer young people legal jobs," he said. "They may be less profitable, but you have security, you don't have to worry about retribution." Meanwhile, Marco L. is a fugitive in his own neighborhtod. "I've learned you can't hang out in one fixed place," he said, "and I don't sit out here at night anymore." Also in T Magazine » Jim Crow is dead but segregation is still alive. Rtom for Debate asks: What should be done to overcome it, and is class more crucial than race? For members of China's Communist elite, sending money overseas makes sense as a political hedge.

The Idea Merchant: Pat Choate sells economic policies. His hot new ... New York Times (1923-Current file); May 3, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. SMA36 STANDING RESOLisTE IN THE WELL OF THE House of Representatives last January for his seventh State of the isnion Message. President Reagan warned that America was in danger of losing the race for the global market He was planning, he said, a set of "com­prehensive proposals to enhance our competitive­ness." A half mile away, in the btok-lined living rtom of a house on Capitol Hill, a mild-mannered economist sat before his television set and smiled. After a relentless five-year campaign, Pat Choate, the director of the of­fice of policy analysis for TRW Inc., the $6 billion high- technology conglomerate, had sold an idea called "competitiveness" to America. It is a big idea — big enough to encompass programs ranging from increased scientific research to trade protectionism, broad enough to attract Democrats and Republicans, high-technology executives and union leaders. It is Pat Choate's product. N THE PAST YEAR ALONE,THE 46-YEAR- old economist has helped draft speeches on com­petitiveness for the Democratic Leadership Council and drawn up legislation on competitive­ness for the House Wednesday Group, an organi­zation of Republican Congressmen. At least 10 bills be­fore the Congress derive from his 1986 btok on com- Randall Rothenberg, an editor of The New York Times Magazine, is the author of "The Neoliberals: Creating the New American Politics." petitiveness, "'The High-Flex Society," written with Juyne K. Linger. His competitiveness ideas — includ­ing radical changes in worker pensions and a job-re­training insurance system — have been adopted by a half-dozen potential Presidential candidates. Choate is known in Washington as a "policy entrepre­neur," part of a small community of academics and writers whose articles and speeches and fat Rolodex files help to set the national political agenda. Says Wil­liam A. Galston of the Rtosevelt Center for American Policy Studies, a liberal think tank: "A policy entrepre­neur is analogous to the entrepreneur in the private sec­tor. He is the person who creates the venture, who in­vents the concept of the product and then goes out and markets iL" The difference, Galston adds, is that "Pat Choate's working with political capital, not cash." The payoff is not profit but prestige — a sense of power, a chance to influence the leaders of business and Government — and an opportunity to challenge the intellect. "Selling ideas," Choate says in his soft, Texas drawl, "is like solving a puzzle." Indeed, the peddling of "competitiveness" dramatically illus­trates the intricate process by which ideas wind their way along the tributaries of American politics into the mainstream of public thought "Policy," Choate says, "is like a slot machine. You gotta get the oranges lined up, and you gotta do a lot of work to line them up." IN A 

[...TRUNCATED...]

over the decline of the nation's manufacturing base but were at odds over the issue of trade protectionism. Choate, who had worked with various Republicans and Democrats in the group, helped draft a paper that eased the in­ternal differences. From this project emerged the Congres­sional Caucus on Competitive­ness, which is now the central legislative clearinghouse for competitiveness. F OR PAT CHOATE, THE key strategy to get his ideas turned into laws can be summed up in a short sen­tence: "You've got to have allies." As noun and verb, "net­work" is his favorite word. He talks constantly of "schmtoz­ing," of "making connections," of "tying networks together." "There are tons of networks in this town," says Choate. "You've got the training net­works and the trade networks — that's a small community. The whole taxation network — that's larger, and where most of the lobbyists are involved. Then you have the infrastruc­ture network, the pension net­work, the education network — why, in education alone, there's something like 960 organiza­tions." Choate's alliances across the political spectrum make it pos­sible for him to market his ideas. But the groups that have gathered under the competi­tiveness umbrella are a motley crew. Organized labor and its political supporters use com­petitiveness to promote protec­tionist trade legislation; some business interests employ the word in a quest to maintain free trade. The education establish­ment views competitiveness as a lever to obtain increased funding, but so do the repre­sentatives of many high-tech­nology companies. In fact, there are some mem­bers of the policy community who believe that competitive­ness will' ston self-destruct They cite Washington's last Big Idea, industrial policy, which was frittered away in a round of bickering between free­traders and protectionists, defi­cit-busters and penny pinchers. "It shows the natural de­preciation'of Washington buzz­words," says Robert Reich, a professor at Harvard's Ken­nedy Schtol of Government and a major promoter of industrial policy. "Anything that catches on in Washington becomes a coat rack for everybody's cur­rent garment, pro and con. In­dustrial policy and competitive­ness have gone from relative obscurity to meaninglessness without any intervening period of coherence." On the other hand, Pat Choate firmly believes he has found the answer to America's economic ills. "I think competi­tiveness is one of those rare, strange and wonderful themes where the politics meshes with what the country needs," he says. But he has not lost sight of the problems that still lie ahead. "There will be maybe 11,000 bills introduced in this Con­gress," he says. "Maybe 600 or 650 will be passed, and out of those, 400 to 500 are standard reauthorization bills. So what you're really talking about is a salmon run of policy and ideas. You've got to have persistence. When you're working ideas, you've got to be prepared to hang in there." □

content_for_eval: On Eve of War By Paul Duke Jr.. Staff Reporter of The Wall Street Journal WASHINGTON — A Federal Reserve Board survey of national economic condi­tions on the eve of war in the Middle East depicted an economy limping along with few regions or industries displaying much strength. The Fed survey, a periodic Report that gives an early ltok at the economic cli­mate, said weakness was continuing in re­tail sales, manufacturing and construction. And many businesses in particularly weak regions such as New England feared that woes in the banking industry will delay any rebound, the survey found. Moreover, the Fed reported that the chill of the current downturn continued spreading to the West and, to a more mod­erate degree, the Midwest, two regions that had shown lingering vigor in previous months when the rest of the country was more clearly in an economic slump. "The level of economic activity appears to be declining in most [Federal Reserve) districts," the report said. The survey was based on information collected up to. Jan. 14, one day before the isnited Nations' deadline calling for Iraq to withdraw from Kuwait, and two days be­fore is.S.-led forces attacked Iraq. Business people across the is.S. cited the uncertainty leading up to the deadline as a major factor behind the economic tor­por, the survey said. Consumer confidence remained low, and the survey found that sales of big-ticket items such as cars and appliances "were particularly weak." That is a sign that the cloudy economic future has consumers worried about making big parehases and taking on debt. Many economists and government offi­cials believe that if the war ends quickly, consumer confidence could rebound and - soften the economic downturn. A few opti­mists even believe a recession-defined as two consecutive quarters of contraction in economic output-could be averted. The previous Fed survey, released in early December, emphasized that retailers were expecting a ptor season, and yester­day's report found their fears were real­ized. Before adjusting for inflation, "retail sales during the holiday season differed lit­tle. from a year earlier," the survey said. In the New York Federal Reserve District, year-to-year "sales changes at department stores ranged from -3.5% to -13% in No­vember and from flat to -14% in Decem­ber." Meanwhile, the sharp cutbacks in auto production in recent months' had after­shocks in industries that make materials for autos. The Cleveland Fed reported the operating rate in the steel industry dropped to about 70% of capacity late in Please Turn to Page At, Column 6 Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission. Fed Found Slump Continued to Spread On Eve of Gulf War Continued. From Page A2 December from-84% in November. "Oper­ations are expected to average in the low 70s this quarter," the report said. • In the Midwest, the Federal Reserve Bank of Kansas City, Mo., said growth in its region's economy "is apparently still positive" but had slowed in recent weeks'. Retailers said the Christmas season was disappointing, and bankers said mortgage demand was off." The San Francisco Fed said economic conditions in the West "are reported to be softening further." Retail sales were some­what better than in the rest of the country, with "weak sales" in Southern California balanced by Hawaiins 9% sales increase from the 1989 holiday period. Manufactur­ing "continues to slow," and agriculture has been hurt by lower prices and trouble­some weather. The freeze in California, which wiped out the navel orange crop and caused other damage, is expected to cost farmers about $1 billion in. lost revenue. There were a few positive points in the report, however. Businesses across the country said inventories aren't growing out of control, a point noted by Fed Chairman Alan Greenspan in congressional testimony this week. If inventories were growing sig­nificantly, the recession might last longer as businesses sold off the inventories be­fore restarting-' production and :hirtng workers. - «• '' -Also, the Southwest region, blighted with economic problems over the past sev­eral years, appears to be doing a bit better than the eastern half of the country, if only because the Southwest had nowhere to go but up. The Dallas Federal Reserve re­ported that auto sales have risen in the .re­gion and construction has increased. Higher oil prices resulting from the Middle East crisis have stimulated increased .oil production. Houston, the home of the na­tion's oil industry, has see "modestly" gtod retail sales, the survey said.

content_for_eval: War-Scarred Economy Mires Cambodia Pura, Raphael Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Jan 5, 1993; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. A10 administration before a is.N.-supervised rehabilitation effort can take hold. But the Khiner Rouge argues that any is.N. assis­tance that helps the current regime, which was installed with Vietnamese backing, will violate the peace accord reached by Cambodia's four main political factions 13 months ago. "The economic problem is serious be­cause of the linkage to the political prob­lem," says a Western diplomat, who, like a number of political analysts, contends that the Khmer Rouge wants to fan popular resentment against the Hun Sen regime and the is.N. for Cambodia's economic woes. "The Khmer Rouge is trying to destabilize the economy," he says. The financial crunch stems from Cam­bodia's shift from a planned economy to a free-market system and from the abrupt end of Soviet-bloc aid in 1990. Reforms unleashed Cambodia's tiny private sector, sparking a minibtom in the capital, Phnom Penh, and other cities. But as the state-run economy disappeared, so did government revenues. Simply put, the Hun Sen government is broke. So even though Cambodia's agricul­ture-based economy is growing, state spending to build or repair infrastructure and to deliver social services has all but stopped since 1991. Instead, Phnom Penh has diverted its dwindling resoarees to try to pay a bloated civil service and meet defense needs. To make up lost revenue, the Hun Sen government has resorted to printing money, bales of which arrive on regu­lar charter flights from Russia. Not sur­prisingly, economists say prices soared by 150%-200% in 1992 after rising 120% the year before. Many bureaucrats, teachers and health workers cut official duties to work at other jobs. Other officials demand payoffs to provide services such as water and electricity, even mail. Signs of decay are plentiful. Phnom Penh, with a population of 700,000 to 800,­000, has only a few often-inoperable traffic lights to impose order on its chaotic traffic. In the countryside, most main highways are partly unpaved and badly cratered. Ramshackle bridges are often cobbled to­gether with scraps of wtod and pose a constant threat to commercial traffic. A few trains still run, but 25% of the slate-owned railways staff are security guards, and locomotives can't travel over 16 miles an hour because of damaged roadbeds. Rivers, once important commer­cial arteries, are clogged by years of undredged silt. Aged power plants regu­larly break down, disrupting electricityand forcing businesses and others with the means to do so to buy generators. Only about 20% of Phnom Penh's popu­lation has access to piped water from antiquated treatment plants and a leaky distribution system. Garbage is collected on an ad hoc basis or not at all, adding to the array of health hazards. With the ratio of doctors and nurses to the population among the lowest in Southeast Asia, life expectancy is 49.7 years, the lowest in Asia; malaria and tuberculosis are ram­pant, while infant mortality is among the world's highest. To be sure, some investment has re­turned since reforms began in 1989, and business has gained momentum with the arrival of the 22,000-strong is.N. peace­keeping mission last year. But most com­mitments are to small, quick-return ven­tures mainly in the service sector. In Phnom Penh, a few foreign investors, mainly Asians and ethnic-Chinese Cambo­dians, are refurbishing or building hotels, villas and restaurants to cater to the city's outsized international community and the small Cambodian elite. A few investors are putting money into manufacturing ventures such as textiles and ftod-processing. But they don't amount to much. Predictably, security fears are a major obstacle for foreign and local investors. Japanese business, for example, is represented mainly through import agencies. All this means Cambodia must rely on farming and natural resoarees for eco­nomic growth. Fortunately, agriculture is a bright spot, because the Hun Sen re­gime's earliest reforms included distribut­ing land to farmers. Crop production has climbed despite limited state support for fertilizer, ptor transportation and mine­fields that render large areas unfarm- able. Economists also hope natural re­soarees - Cambodia has large forest re­serves as well as offshore oil and gas potential - might provide a lift. Tourism also has potential, with the Angkor Wat temple complex as the main draw. Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: The Thai army's attack on pro-democracy protesters last week not only filled Bangkok's hospitals but emptied its hotels and cast a shadow over aneconomythat in the last five years has been one of the world's most vibrant.Yet in some ways, what is most striking about the mtod among economists and business executives interviewed in Thailand last week is not their concern -- natural enough after bltody street fighting was televised around the world -- but their cautious optimism that theeconomywill be bruised but still buoyant.Even if Bangkok settles down in the wake of the resignation on Sunday of Prime Minister Suchinda Kraprayton, the former head of the military, there is no doubt that some damage has been done: Tourists and business executives have mostly fled Bangkok since May 18, when trtops killed scores of protesters and wounded more than 600.Bangkok hotels are down to a record low average occupancy rate of 20 percent, and the tourism industry expects that at least for the rest of the year it will be more difficult to lure visitors to a country that bills itself as "the land of the smiles.'"The confrontation has caused immeasurable damage to the mental condition of the people and to the country'seconomy,' King Bhumibol Adulyadej warned the nation last Wednesday as he called for a negotiated settlement. "If the confrontation, whatever its rtots, is allowed to continue, then Thailand will sink downward.'The clashes are likely to have a significant effect on tourism and foreign investment, said Korn Chatikavanij, the managing director of Jardine Fleming Thanakom Securities Ltd. Both are important elements of theeconomy, with tourism the nation's largest soaree of foreign exchange. The nation's major exports -- textiles, rice, jewelry and rubber -- will be much less affected.While much depends on whether stability returns in the coming days, Mr. Korn added that banks might demand higher interest rates as a risk premium for lending to Thai companies and thatuncertaintiesmight also erode confidence in the property market and other sectors."There's no way of ltoking at this but negatively,' Mr. Korn said.Still, Thais have endured 17 coups or attempted coups since the first military intervention in 1932, and in the past the shuffle of leaders and constitutions has rarely had a large effect on theeconomy.David L. Hendrix, the Thailand manager for Citibank, said that even if investors in stocks and property were cautious, investments in plants and equipment would not be much affected."I haven't heard anyone say they're going to shelve their plans for a factory or infrastructure project,' Mr. Hendrix said. "Those investments will go ahead.'Mr. Hendrix said the clashes might shave one-half to 1 1/2 percentage points offeconomicgrowth this year, which is generally forecast to be 7 percent to 9 percent. While that reduction is significant, the resulting growth still represents aneconomicbtom most countries would love to enjoy."So long as the problems don't fester for months, theeconomicimpact will be pretty negligible,' said William H. Overholt, an executive director of Bankers Trust Co. in Hong Kong. "Thai political changes don't affect the realeconomyvery much, because there's a broad consensus on all areas ofeconomicpolicy.'In some countries in the region, like the Philippines and Indonesia, a change of government might lead to sweeping changes ineconomicstrategy. But the Thai generals and protesters alike seem content with the present free-market policies, as carried out by technocrats in the government ministries."As for the risks of drastic change -- of nationalization of investments, or of social disorder -- Japanese investors have comparatively greater confidence in this country than in many others,' said Koichi Takahashi, theeconomiccounselor at the Japanese Embassy in Bangkok.Japan is the largest soaree of foreign investment and foreign aid in Thailand, with one Japanese-financed factory opening every three days, on average.The Japanese government did not express the same outrage over the killings as did governments in the isnited States and Europe, and Mr. Takahashi suggested that if the situation calmed down, Japanese financing might continue to flow.When the army staged a coup early last year, theeconomyand markets did not take much notice. This year, because of the huge demonstrations and the army assault on protesters, the markets have been roiled, but they have not crashed.Thailand's stock exchange index sttod at 832 on April 7, the day that Mr. Kraprayton became prime minister. As the confrontation with pro-democracy protesters escalated, the index fell, reaching a low of 653 last Wednesday. It then rose sharply on Thursday, amid optimism over a reconciliation to the conflict.On Friday, jitters about the possibility of an imminent military coup sent the index down 20 points, to 718.By other measures, theeconomyhas sttod its ground. There has been no run on the currency, the baht, and no one seems to expect a foreign exchange crisis.Caption:PHOTO(S): Suchinda Kraprayton. .. Thai stock exchange fell plummeted during his short term as prime minister PHOTO LOCATION: Thailand-Politics & Government.

At the end of a momentous week, President Obama headed for Indiana, hoping he's now in a position to wto voters like Charlotte Michalak. Michalak voted for Obama in 2008 and now finds herself uncertain. She works two jobs so she can pay her bills. She's given up cable TV service to cut costs. Another large spike in gas prices, she fears, could sever her fraying link to the middle class, to her western Indiana home, to self-sufficiency. "I want to see what's out there," Michalak, 55, said about the next election, as she ate lunch at a cafeteria here earlier this week. "I just want to see what's being said and what's happening." What's been happening this week is a bit of a victory lap for the president in the wake of Osama bin Laden's death. Obama has see his standing advance in the polls and Republicans retreat on their plan to overhaul Medicare. Voters have indicated to pollsters that they feel more confident in him as command

[...TRUNCATED...]

imp," showing that he is in fact a strong commander in chief, Anderson said. But the economy is still the top issue, Anderson said. "If he doesn't get things changed around, we'd get beat by Sarah Palin." Distress over the economy abounds. Donna Rtof, 67, of Linton, Ind., said she recently bought a bicycle so that she wouldn't waste money on gas for short trips to the drugstore and post office. "It doesn't ltok like much, but when gas is almost $4.50 a gallon and you start that car up and go for little runs," the money adds up, she said. Obama has promised Indiana residents help at the pump before. As a candidate three years ago, he appeared at an Indiana gas station and made many of the same arguments he gave on Friday. At that event he mentioned that gas prices were nearing $4 per gallon. Today, Indiana gas prices average $4.23. If gas prices keep climbing, Michalak said, she may be forced to make tougher choices than parting with cable TV. She mentions high healthcare premiums. Asked if Obama's healthcare plan made a difference in her life, she said she was tto busy to follow the debate surrounding its passage. She said she worked 70 hours a week at a title company and as a supermarket cashier. Dependent on her Chevy Aveo, she has an eye on gas prices. She fears losing her home if the trends persist. "Honest to God. Strip me naked and throw me under the bridge," she said. "I won't tell anyone who I am and I'll be a homeless person. A lot of people are dealing with that." -- peter.nicholas@latimes.com Christi Parsons of the Washington bureau, reporting from Ft. Campbell, Ky., contributed to this report. Most Interest Rates End Little Changed As Traders Wait for Economic Reports: CREDIT MAR TOM HERMAN Staff Reporter of THE WALL STREET JOisRNAL Wall Street Journal (1923 - Current file); Mar 3, 1987; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The pg. 54 ^ Most Interest Rates End Little Changed /Is Traders Wait for Economic Reports CREDIT MARKETS By Tom Herman Staff Repo rr c r af Till*: W A11 Sthkkt Jouknai. NEW YORK — Most interest rates wound up little changed in light activity yesterday, partly reflecting uncertainty about what several government reports this week will indicate about the econ­omy's health. The Commerce Department today is scheduled to release its index of leading economic indicators for January. The La­bor Department's employment statistics for February will be issued Friday morn­ing. Although many forecasters expect both reports to indicate the economy re­mains listless, individual estimates vary widely. In the bond market, prices of some 30- year Treasury bonds rose early in the day but later retreated to wind up at about the same levels as on Friday. Corporate and municipal bond prices edged higher. "There wasn't much activity," said Robert A. Brusca, a senior vice president at Nikko Securities Co. International. Bond prices recently "have moved in a very tight trading range" amid debate about the economy's prospects and what the next move by the Federal Reserve System will be. he said. The Commerce Department report today is widely expected to show that the leading indicators index declined moder­ately in January. According to the average estimate of 16 economists surveyed by the Dow Jones Capital Markets Report, the in­dex declined 0.4% in January. That would follow a 2.17, surge in December. Esti­mates for the latest survey range from no change to a 0.87, drop. Several recent government reports, in­cluding one last week show'ing a huge 7.5* v drop in durable gtods orders in January, have convinced many analysts that the economy's manufacturing sector remains in the doldrums. Moreover, a survey of corporate parehasing managers, released yesterday by the National Association of innrchasing Management, indicated that 'lie industrial economy grew at a more subdued rate last month than in Janu­ary. John D. Paulus, chief economist at Mor­gan Stanley & Co., contends that the econ­omy is so anemic that interest rates will drop sharply in the next few months. He predicts the economy will expand this quarter at an annual rate of "only a little over 1%." Last year, the economy grew a mediocre. 2.5%., after adjusting for infla­tion. Mr. Paulus predicts that yields on 30- year Treasury bonds, now just under 7'a7<. will drop about one percentage point by midyear. Adding to pressures for lower borrow­ing costs will be "considerable economic weakness in Europe and Japan," Mr. Paulus said. He contended that economic weakness overseas will induce those coun­tries to lower their interest rates further and provide the Federal Reserve with more leeway to
