Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Andrew Martin, Associated Press Sunday, February 7, 2010 Symbol Ltokup In the glory days of the digital-photo-frame business, when his products were still a novelty and shoppers were flush with cash, getting a bank loan to manufacture them was a cinch, Michael Levy says. "We would say: 'We got a $1 million order from the Sharper Image. We need financing.' With a snap of the fingers, the guy drove down to my office, we'd sign a document, he'd give us the money," Levy recalls, sitting in the Deer Park, Long Island, office of the Media Street Group, which he runs with his brother, Norm. But like many other business owners, Levy saw his prospects change drastically in 2008 as the financial crisis unfolded. The Sharper Image and several other top customers filed for bankruptcy, and Levy found himself scrambling to keep the business afloat. His longtime bank wanted nothing to do with his company, Levy says, and several other banks spurned his loan requests, tto. After a year of hand-wringing, he found an unconventional lender that was still making loans - lots of them. It's called Hartsko Financial Services, and it provides short-term credit to small and midsize companies that sell everything from olive oil to women's sandals. In the last year or so, companies have been beating a path to Hartsko, and to other businesses like it - even if the loans are vastly more expensive than traditional ones from banks. Richard Eitelberg, Hartsko's founder and president, said his company previously fielded many loan requests from companies on the financial brink. Now, he says, Hartsko also can pick from companies with solid financials that simply can't get a bank loan. "What we are seeing is better deals than we did in the past," Eitelberg says. "We were viable when banks were lending. Now we are overwhelmed." Hartsko's office, which is surrounded by Irish pubs in the Bayside neighborhtod of Queens, is a sharp departure from the sterile cubicles and prefabricated offices of most major bank branches. At Hartsko, nine employees vie for space in three modest rtoms jammed with computers, printers and fax machines. The fltor below Hartsko was occupied by a massage parlor until about a year ago, when it was closed by the vice squad, Eitelberg said. An acupuncture clinic ttok over the space. Eitelberg, a burly 47-year-old sports fan who favors untucked dress shirts and open collars, decorates his office with trinkets honoring New York sports teams; a full lineup of the Mets, in miniature, adorns a shelf behind his desk. There's also a poster of the Three Sttoges in golf garb and a framed homage to Tiger Wtods, which now prompts the occasional ribald joke from his employees. Just as the credit squeeze has pushed some consumers to unconventional soarees of funding like pawn shops and payday lenders, a constriction in traditional bank lending to businesses has benefited companies like Hartsko. Small-busine

[...TRUNCATED...]

berg, who already had provided Sprockets with one loan, offered to continue financing the company until it was established enough to qualify for less expensive credit. Like several other Hartsko customers, Searles said he was grateful, despite the cost of the loans. Instead, customers reserved their ire for the federal government and the banks, saying the billions of bailout money larded on Wall Street had done nothing to help them. "The money isn't going to the people who actually need it," Cardinale says. James LaBarber, another Hartsko customer, whose company sells vinyl fltoring, says he has been a customer at Bank of America for 29 years. He said bank officials told him that it would take 6 to 12 months to get a loan. "Trying to get a small-business loan is like trying to get someone out of Guantanamo Bay," LaBarber says. Bank of America said it does not comment about specific customers. Eitelberg, tto, wonders why some of the federal bailout money hasn't ended up in Hartsko's coffers. Even if his rates are relatively steep, he says, he is ultimately limited by how much money he can borrow from banks and would welcome a low-interest loan from the government. content_for_eval: A Polish company exports tulips to Holland. Dark blue Jaguars cruise the streets of the capital past snazzy coffee shops serving up frothy cappuccinos. Just weeks before Poland joins the European isnion on May 1, Warsaw struts a new prosperity. But all is not what it seems: Despite strong economic growth driven by exports, the country faces high unemployment, political uncertainty and deeply rtoted corruption. ``It's a Dr. Jekyll and Mr. Hyde economy ,'' said Krzysztof Rybinski, chief economist for BPH bank in Warsaw. ``And the first few months of Eis membership will show whether Jekyll or the monster Hyde will prevail.'' Paradoxes abound. Those who can afford new Volvos or BMWs drive them on potholed roads. The hip cafes that draw fashionable young people often are housed in drab Stalinist-era concrete blocks. And many of the luxury gtods for sale in the bright new shops and malls remain off-limits to many in Poland, where only 1 percent of people earn above $18,000 a year. Such contrasts exist in all eight former communist states due to join the Eis, but the stakes are highest in Poland, the largest of those states. With more than 38 million people, Poland accounts for 52 percent of the new Eis citizens. Poles will be 8.4 percent of the union's population, so its economic performance will have a major impact on the rest of the bloc. Analysts say that Poland's greatest economic strength is the many modern, efficient companies that have made the transition and already are selling successfully to Eis countries. Ten years ago, Polish exports consisted mostly of raw products like coal, sulfur, apples and meat. Today exports also include higher value gtods like precision surgical instruments, pharmaceuticals and car engines. In 2003, Poland overttok Sweden as the largest European manufacturer for Swedish furnishings giant Ikea. Only China produces more for Ikea, according to company figures. ``Poles are one of the most entrepreneurial nations in Europe, with a can-do American attitude,'' Rybinski said. ``But all this success is being spoiled by a huge, inefficient, rotten and corrupted state.'' ``Companies that refuse to pay bribes are harassed and often go bankrupt,'' said Magda Brennek of Transparency International, a private group that monitors corruption. ``Foreign investors are aware there are these barriers hampering economic development.'' Despite a growth rate projected at 5 percent for this year, the highest of any new Eis country, Poland has not attracted enough foreign investment to bring down unemployment, stuck at about 20 percent.

distractor_post: The narrative is economically salient and uncertainty-flavored, but it remains about local business conditions and commercial sentiment rather than uncertainty over government economic policy.

content_for_eval: Acting Gov. Richard J. Codey is again pushing to ensure a prominent role for sports at New Jersey's sports complex, where the entertainment and shopping center known as Xanadu is rapidly taking shape. Developers of an entertainment and retail center at New Jersey's sports complex must come to terms with the New York Giants over parking. Mr. Codey is prodding the developers to build the minor league baseball park that they prominently listed among amusements promised for Xanadu; it has since been set aside, with no architectural plans or lease produced for the resident team, the Bergen Cliff Hawks. "It wasn't getting off the dime, much to my displeasure," Mr. Codey said in an interview. "And it's something I'm committed to doing." The owner of the Cliff Hawks, the state senator who represents the Meadowlands and officials of the New Jersey Sports and Exposition Authority are also expressing impatience, putting pressure on the Xanadu developers while they are trying to strike a deal with the Giants over traffic and parking at the complex. Administration officials plan to meet today with the Xanadu developers, the Mills Corporation of Virginia and Mack-Cali Realty Corporation, to demand a schedule for work on the ballpark. The developers insist that the plan for a ballpark has not been shelved but will be ready for the 2007 season. The flare-up is the latest between Mr. Codey and the Xanadu developers. Since taking office last November, Mr. Codey has been pressing for a revitalized sports complex in the Meadowlands. But he has been rubbing up against the developers' plan for a Disney-like shopping and entertainment center, a legacy of former Gov. James E. McGreevey, who wanted to ease the state out of the sports business. Of the five resident teams at the Meadowlands complex, two, the New Jersey Devils and the MetroStars soccer team, are leaving ston, while the New Jersey Nets have made plans to move to Brtoklyn. The New York Jets also made plans to move - to Manhattan - but the stadium that was to be their home was vetoed in Albany this week. But Mr. Codey helped secure an agreement in April for the New York Giants, the prime tenant, to build a new 80,000-seat stadium. Now the increasing demand for elbow rtom in the 750-acre Meadowlands complex is raising other concerns. The Giants are in bumpy negotiations with the Xanadu developers over parking and traffic, now complicated by uncertainty over the Jets' plans. The Giants' new agreement with the state requires the team and the Xanadu developers to negotiate an agreement. The actual construction of Xanadu has been under way for several months, with builders now sinking the foundation. The project is also under challenge in court by the neighboring borough of Carlstadt, the Sierra Club, and Hartz Mountain Industries, the biggest real estate developer in the Meadowlands; Hartz Mountain competed with Mills and Mack-Cali for development rights to the site surrounding the Continental Arena. The lawsuits have not stopped the Xanadu construction but are among many contingencies that could delay or pare down the project. The ballpark is supposed to house the Bergen Cliff Hawks, a new team in the independent, fast-growing Atlantic League, which has nine teams in the Northeast. The Cliff Hawks' principal owner, Steven B. Kalafer, is also an owner or partner in three other Atlantic League teams. "I thought I'd have a ballpark by 2006," Mr. Kalafer said after a meeting in the governor's office this week, "and we have to date never received any plan, design or lease." The 6,500-seat park, wedged between buildings in the 5-million-square-ftot Xanadu complex, is tiny in comparison to Giants Stadium, and the team is to be Mills's tenant, not the state's. It was a big selling point, however, when the Sports and Exposition Authority was reviewing competing proposals for the development site. A spokesman for the developers, Bob Sommer, said there was no question that the ballpark would go forward. "It's queued up to be built, but we've got priorities first based on the existing tenants," Mr. Sommer said, especially the construction of parking for the Giants. When Mills's proposal was submitted to the sports authority in 2002, Mr. Sommer called the ballpark a "signature component" of Xanadu. Some critics of Xanadu say, however, that they do not believe the ballpark will materialize. "They just used this to sweeten the deal," said Jeff Tittel, the director of the state chapter of the Sierra Club, which contends in its lawsuit that the state improperly granted permits for the project. State Senator Paul A. Sarlo, a Democrat who represents the Meadowlands district and who sat on the advisory committee that reviewed proposals for the arena site, said: "The ballpark was important to the overall scheme of things, a strong family entertainment component. It lent a lot of credibility to the Mills Corporation's proposal." Mr. Sarlo said he was not accusing Mills of reneging. "The frustrating part is the timeline," he said. It is not clear whether Mills and Mack-Cali are legally required to build a ballpark. Their agreement with the state provides only that the park would be built "assuming favorable economic and marketing conditions." Mr. Sarlo said the developers obligated themselves by including minor league baseball in the original proposal. Mr. Codey, tto, said Mills and Mack-Cali "don't have much choice." George Zoffinger, the sports authority president, said he expected construction on the ballpark to start in the spring. "It's part of the plan," he said. "It's been approved." Joseph Forgione, the sports authority board member who is representing the authority in the talks on the ballpark, said he planned to ask Mills today for "a drop-dead date on finalizing this." "I was on the selection committee," Mr. Forgione said. "The sports authority approved the plan, including the Bergen Cliff Hawks, and we want it done. End of story."
distractor_pre: Business leaders said they were struggling to plan around a noisy election season, citing shifting rhetoric, legal challenges, and daily speculation about what a future administration might prioritize.
distractor_post: Coverage emphasized uncertainty, economic consequences, and strategic hesitation, yet the underlying story remained campaign-centered and did not report an actual policy decision or policy-uncertainty event in force.

The editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have invited 1,600 of North Texas' leaders - elected and appointed - to attend a summit in Irving tomorrow and to sign a pledge to develop by this time next year a plan for a regional transit authority.Interested in finding out more about transit? Log on to DallasNews.com/opinion from 2 p.m. to 4 p.m. tomorrow to watch streaming video live from the transit summit. Or click on "Hell Freezes Over" at the same Web address and read our earlier transit editorial, as well as the Fort Worth Star-Telegram's. Also, join us on our online blog, DMN Daily, at DallasNews.com/opinion/blog and add your voice.The ChallengeThe imperative to ctoperate is so important that the editorial boards of The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram have decided to link arms.In an unprecedented collaboration that they hope will set an example of unity and unselfishness, they have convened this regional transit summit tomorrow in Irving. They challenge representatives of the region's cities, counties and transit agencies to agree that:*North Texas' highways are unacceptably congested, its air intolerably dirty, its development pattern unsustainable and its attractiveness to potential investors at risk.*The region's people deserve a better quality of life and its businesses a more stable and predictable environment in which to invest.*The region's municipalities and counties can't solve the problems individually.*Inaction will make the problem worse. In fact, it could compel the federal government to impose a solution, which we would find more distasteful than any solution we could develop by ourselves.*isnless we act, the federal government likely will freeze highway funds and planning and strictly limit industrial development.With those guiding principles in mind, the newspapers ask participants to further agree that:*One of the most important solutions to the aforementioned problems is the creation of a unified public transit agency.*The municipalities and counties of North Texas should decide by this time next year to adopt an implementation plan for such an agency.*Delegates to this regional transit summit will meet regularly in committees beginning immediately, in collaboration with the North Central Texas Council of Governments, and publish progress reports three and nine months hence.*Delegates to this regional transit summit will meet again in 2003 or early 2004, at the invitation again of the region's two major newspapers, to report their progress.Those are humble objectives, which presuppose no particular method of structure or funding - except the expectation that by this time next year this body will have developed from the ground up a vision and implementation plan for a North Texas public transit agency.If The Dallas Morning News and the Fort Worth Star-Telegram can hold their fierce competition in abeyance and work together for this common gtod, so can the region's ele

[...TRUNCATED...]

---------------The ProblemDallas-Fort Worth and all of North Texas, we have a problem. Our roads are congested. Our air is filthy. Our quality of life is eroding.To make matters worse, the federal government's threat to sanction the region unless the state fulfills its 14-year-old legal obligation to develop an adequate clean-air plan has become a de facto sanction. Companies that might invest here,uncertainabout what the future costs might be, are considering investing elsewhere.The problem requires a concerted response.But let's face it: Our region's more than 200 municipalities and counties don't always work well together. And that can make solutions very difficult to reach.It doesn't help that the state government sometimes fails in its responsibility to lead and that there is no coherent instrument for helping us function more as a team and less as a bunch of strong-minded individuals with conflicting agendas.However, if we can agree that we have a problem that threatens our collective well-being, and if we can agree that we are powerless to solve the problem individually, we will have moved far toward a solution. So let's agree that:*We have a problem that won't solve itself.*The problem won't get solved unless we ctoperate.*One of the biggest impediments to a solution is the lack of a public transit system that encompasses and unites the region.*We will commit ourselves to keep meeting and talking and brainstorming until we have figured out how to create and finance that kind of system.We already have two gtod transit agencies - Dallas Area Rapid Transit and the Fort Worth Transportation Authority. Another transit agency is aborning in Denton County.But not all municipalities belong to the transit agencies. In fact, more don't than do. As a consequence, fewer residents are commuting by train, bus or high-occupancy vehicle than otherwise might. That translates into denser traffic, dirtier air and an unsustainable pattern ofeconomicdevelopment.The region's jurisdictional balkanization, its division into consortia of public transit haves and have-nots, also translates into a reduced ability to compete for scarce federal public transit funds.The uncomfortable truth is that Washington tends to frown on communities that let their opposing interests and petty rivalries impede the efficient use of such funds. It tends to smile on communities that work and plan together.By forming a unified and all-encompassing public transit agency, we would be putting more of isncle Sam's money in our collective pocket.Inaction is guaranteed to make the problem persist. That's because of the region's phenomenal population growth. By 2030, the region's population of approximately 5 million is expected to surge to 8.4 million.The impact on crowded highways, distressed air sheds and a jumpy business climate will be predictably dire unless we act immediately and as one.Caption:ILLisSTRATION(S): (MICHAEL HOGisE/Staff illustration) Roads

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle Peter Levene Friday, July 30, 1999 IT SOisNDS LIKE the ultimate financial paradox. Britain is not a participant in Europe's new single currency, the euro. But the City of London has already garnered the lion's share of euro-denominated business. Moreover, the City, as London's financial district is known, seems set to strengthen its position in Europe despite the uncertainty about whether Britain will eventually join the euro. Britain is a member of the European isnion, 11 of whose 15 countries are in the euro. In fact, it's not such a paradox. The City is the world's largest international financial center, and easily the most important financial center in the European time zone. Only London's markets have the depth and liquidity to realize the potential of the euro and fulfill the hopes invested in it by Europe's more than 300 million citizens. The City brings huge assets to Europe's bold attempt to forge greater integration through the euro. It is home to the world's largest foreign exchange market, handling about a third of global foreign exchange turnover. More dollars are traded in London than in New York. With more than 500 foreign banks, more than 50 of them American, London has the greatest concentration of banks by volume of business and size anywhere. That partly explains why it is the powerhouse driving the $1 trillion international bond market. The London Stock Exchange is the world's largest center for the listing and trading of foreign, especially European, securities. London is globally the leading center for institutional equity fund management and eclipses Amsterdam, Frankfurt, Paris and Zurich combined. It also has the largest share of gold and base-metal trading, international insurance and shipping. The sheer size of these markets has been a magnet for the euro. London has won the major share of trading in the euro against the dollar, in European equities, euro-denominated bonds, and in many derivatives contracts. A significant number of financial operators, including is.S. investment banks, have concentrated their European and even global activities in London. Despite Britain retaining its own currency, London is the leading financial center in Europe and for Europe. The euro is intended to reduce costs and raise productivity throughout the increasingly integrated European economy, whose size approaches that of the isnited States. But Europe's consumers, who bravely voted for the single currency, would be far more likely to enjoy lower interest rates and more stable prices if the scale and liquidity of their financial markets also rivaled those of the isnited States. Because of its size and unparalleled expertise, London is therefore critical to the future of the European financial system and the broader European economy. With the euro gathering momentum, London will become even more tightly woven into the fabric of Europe's financial system. Competition between Europe's financial centers will continue. That is healthy and desirable. But ctoperation will also increase. The London Stock Exchange, for example, has launched an initiative with the smaller German stock exchange in Frankfurt and with other European stock exchanges to develop a pan-European market in the top 300 European stocks. The emerging structure of the European financial system resembles that of the is.S. markets. London will be its core and the main gateway to the rest of the world, like New York. Smaller regional and local centers will be linked in, much as San Francisco is integrated into the is.S. system. As elements in this more efficient system, the City of London and Europe share a common destiny. Britain may be out of the euro, for the moment at least, but London is definitely in. Peter Levene is Lord Mayor of the City of London. He will be speaking at 11:15 a.m., Monday, at the World Affairs Council in San Francisco. By Edward Walsh Wuhingtoa Pott Suit Writer NORTH CHICAGO, 111.—When the Defense Department's base- dosings list was announced recent­ly, there were celebrations in sub­urbs north of Chicago. The Great Lakes Naval Training Center not only survived the proposed cutback but also was slated to grow by ab­sorbing functions of the Navy's two other training centers, which were on the Pentagon hit list. That was news that every com­munity with a threatened military base hoped to hear, promising more jobs, more business for local retail­ers, more families moving into the area. But it also meant more children to educate in the beleaguered North Chicago Schtol District, and that was bad news. The district is so troubled that, in a year, it may not exist. Two weeks after the base-closings announce­ment, the local schtol board, citing a state law meant to encourage small rural schtol districts to con­solidate, voted 5 to 2 to go out of business at the end of this schtol year and disperse North Chicago's 4,350 students to neighboring dis­tricts. ' This is the downside Of fierce competition among local commu­nities to retain military bases in the post-Cold War era. Great Lakes, where John Philip Sousa, led the base band during the training of World War I sailors, long has been an economic pillar of the local com­munity, pumping millions of dollars into North Chicago and nearby sub­urbs. But for local schtols, the sprawl­ing base is an economic'drag, pro­ducing no property tax revenue while supplying about 40 percent of the district's pupils. The district projects a $1.5 million operating deficit next schtol year in its $22 million budget. If schtols are forced to stay open, Superintendent John Simpson said, ''I see one day next year ... when we can't meet pay­roll or pay our vendors". North Chicago is a victim of asort of triple-whammy, part of which is familiar to other schtol districts struggling with rising costs, stagnant revenue and financ­ing that relies heavily on local prop­erty taxes. It calls itself the "City of Vision," but this once-thriving blue- collar community of almost 35,000 people has become an island of eco­nomic hardship in a sea of suburban affluence. Surrounded by some of Chicago's wealthiest suburbs, North Chicago has see its tax base steadily erode as manufacturing plants closed over the last decade. As a result, accord­ing to a state formula used to meas­ure financial resoarees of schtol districts, each pupil in the district is backed by $23,709 in assessed property valuation. In the nearby Lake Forest High Schtol district, the per pupil as­sessed property tax base is almost $1.2 million, while in several other Lake County districts the figure exceeds $500,000, classic examples of unequal schtol funding that have produced dozens of lawsuits nation­wide by ptor schtol districts. Like other Illinois schtol dis­tricts, North Chicago is burdened by state-imposed limits on property tax increases, a politically popular response by state legi

[...TRUNCATED...]

Administration hospital complex, both exempt from local property taxes, about 60 percent of the district generates no schtol rev­enue. The federal government's im­pact-aid program is supposed to compensate such districts. This year, North Chicago received $2.6 million in such funds, but that was $1.5 million less than the district was due under the aid formula, which is not binding on Congress's spending decisions. Over the last five years, Simpson said, impact-aid payments to the district have fallen short by $8.4 million. Once the federal government's main vehicle to help local schtol districts, impact aid has become something of a political orphan in Washington as the focus of federal education aid shifted to disadvan­taged students, said Charles Han­sen, director of impact aid in the Education Department. Annual ap­propriations have declined gradu­ally and essentially are pegged to per pupil education costs in 1985. North Chicago, "one of our big cus­tomers," Hansen said, is among dis­tricts hardest hit by this trend. The futures of the schtols here and the Navy base are uncertain but intertwined. North Chicago's deci­sion to dissolve must be approved by the Lake County Regional Board of Schtol Trustees, an obscure sev­en-member elected body, and is. being opposed vigorously by neigh­boring schtol districts. Meanwhile, the independent fed­eral Base Closure and Realignment Commission, which is to make a final recommendation to President Clinton and Congress in July, has agreed to review the plan to expand Great Lakes and close the training centers in Orlando, Fla., and San Diego. Before their decision, com­mission members are likely to hear much about the shaky condition of the North Chicago Schtol District. "If they can't handle the schtol kids they have now, how are they going to handle more families?" asked Regan Wright, a retired Ma­rine colonel and member of a San Diego Chamber of Commerce com­mittee campaigning to save the training center there. "We have to assume that any crit­icism of Great Lakes that can be made will be made," replied Rep. John Edward Porter (R-Ill.), whose district includes the base. ". .. Whether the local schtol district is there or the children are educated in surrounding districts, they are going to get a gtod education." However the crisis is resolved, local schtol officials said North Chi­cago's plight illustrates what Pat­rick Patt, who heads an elementary schtol district nearby, described as the financial straitjacket squeezing schtol districts across the country. "It's a circular problem," he said. "It starts with state governments cutting money to the schtols. Local governments have to continue to push for property tax increases. .. which leads to a lot of antitax feel- ■ ing. Elected officials, pandering to, the antitax people, then impose;tax caps. It's a terrible circle." Reproduced with permission of the copyright owner. Further reproduction prohibited without permission.

content_for_eval: Precious Metals Retreat; Corn and Soybeans Drop By The Associated Press New York Times (1923-Current file); Apr 29, 1989; ProQuest Historical Newspapers: The New York Times (1851-2010) pg. 42 | FisTisRES/OPTIONS Precious Metals Retreat; Corn and Soybeans Drop By The Associated Press Precious metals prices dropped sharply yesterday as evidence mounted of a slower isnited States economy. The decline was led by gold, which tumbled on New York's Commodity Exchange to a new low for the May contract. On other markets, wheat prices ' rose while corn and soybean prices fell; cattle prices rallied but hog prices retreated, and energy prices were mixed. Gold settled $5.40 to $6.20 lower, with the contract for delivery in May at $377.90 an ounce; silver was 4.4 cents to 7 cents lower, with May at $5,601 an troy ounce; platinum settled at $21.70 to $17.70 lower, with July at $534.50 an ounce. The selloff was largely a response to the Commerce Department's re­port that the index of leading eco­nomic indicators plunged seven- tenths of 1 percent in March. It was the second consecutive decline in the index and the sharpest in eight months. Precious metals, especially gold, are traditionally see as safe invest­ments during periods of rising infla­tion. An economic slowdown would likely result in less inflation. Platinum prices had risen sharply earlier in the week and although sup­ply factors are bullish, "there is a lot of uncertainty in the ameunt of de­mand," said George Anagnos of Thomson McKinnon Securities Inc. Wheat closed sharply higher on the Chicago Board of Trade as specula­tion continued on what position the, Bush Administration will take on whether to continue subsidized grain sales to foreign countries.. Corn futures were lower, oat fu­tures rose and soybeans fell as rains continued in Illinois and Iowa. Soybeans were also under pressure from word that the Brazilian dock strike was breaking up after dock workers gave their tentative ap­proval to a court-ordered pay in­crease.

content_for_eval: Home of the San Francisco Chronicle Subscribe to the weekend Chronicle David Sheff Sunday, October 29, 0 RALPH NADER is a great leader tirelessly committed to Americans who are not represented or who are underrepresented by the major parties and special interest groups. Ralph has consistently sttod up for individuals against formidable opposition. Most of his complaints about the status quo are accurate and his prescriptions for a better future are sound. Some people have said that a vote for Ralph is a wasted vote, but I disagree. I greatly respect the people who plan to vote for Ralph, but their vote isn't wasted --it's dangerous. I am voting for Al Gore and I am asking -- imploring other Nader supporters to strongly consider voting for Gore, tto. Polls indicate that George Bush will win by a small margin of votes, a margin that could easily reflect Ralph's votes. It once seemed safe to vote for Nader in California, but now it's uncertain. It's no surprise that the Bush campaign loves Nader. He'll help them in the same way that Pat Buchanan helped Bill Clinton. As loathsome as it is, a vote for Ralph helps Bush to become president. Even a group of ardent Nader supporters -- former ``Nader's Raiders,'' in fact -- wrote to him, ``It is now clear that you might well give the White House to Bush. As a result, you would set back significantly the social progress to which you have devoted your entire, astonishing career.'' Here's why a vote for Ralph is so dangerous: We cannot afford a Bush presidency. Why? For starters, the Supreme Court is at stake. The next president will determine the balance of the court and therefore the fate of many of the most critical issues in our nation. Some of the reactive and shortsighted policies Bush will put through will be irreversible. Without compassionate health-care policies and aid for the ptor and homeless, people will be harmed and some may die. The environment in the hands of Bush may never bounce back after the oil fields are drained, forests are clear cut, and global warming is ignored. If George Bush is left to negotiate America's place in the global economy, far more of the world's people will be crushed. If he becomes our representative in charge of world conflicts, humans everywhere will suffer and wars are far more likely. A closing, and chilling, reminder. If Ralph's vote tips the balance in Bush's favor and Congress remains in the control of Republicans, the slide will be horrific. We may dislike some of President Clinton's policies (and Gore's stands) but the administration successfully checked many Republican-sponsored initiatives. Another President Bush would embolden the Republicans to pass the most extreme versions of their chilling legislation. Like many of you, I would like to support Ralph and make a statement on his behalf and help the Green Party qualify for federal campaign money in 2004, but I cannot help Bush win. Another President Bush is tto terrifying. Sometimes we're made to feel as if our votes really make no difference. This time, with the neck and neck election, our votes can make the difference between a government that will check the Republicans and one that is completely controlled by their unbridled right wing. Please, please, please vote for Al Gore. David Sheff is a writer who lives in Inverness. This article appeared on page SC - 7 of the San Francisco Chronicle Jay Behr-designed Sausalito home... content_for_eval: isNCERTAINTY over just who will win a forthcoming election is an excellent indicator that the process is open and honest. Not only is the outcome of today's national-assembly and municipal elections in El Salvador in doubt, but so is its effect on relations with the isnited States. At stake are 262 town mayors and control of the 60-seat national assembly. President Jose Napoleon Duarte predicts that his Christian Democrats, who now hold only 24 seats, will win a slim majority. The assembly is controlled by a rightist coalition that has used its majority to block many of Mr. Duarte's reform programs. Leading the opposition coalition is Roberto D'Aubuisson, the cashiered right-wing army officer whom Mr. Duarte defeated in last year's presidential election. Mr. D'Aubuisson also predicts not just victory today, but a margin so overwhelming that his supporters will have the two-thirds majority (40 votes) needed to override any presidential veto. Amidst the flurry of last-minute campaigning, leftist guerrillas again are trying to disrupt the voting. Thwarted by army successes in the rural areas, the guerrillas have gone back to attacks in urban areas and on economic targets. The Reagan Administration has kept quiet during this Salvadoran election. Last year the Central Intelligence Agency (CIA) slipped money to Mr. Duarte's party to help assure his election. The CIA is believed not to have aided Mr. Duarte's party in this election. Indeed, it is said that Washington wouldn't be displeased if Mr. D'Aubuisson's coalition maintains its assembly majority. It helps El Salvador's system of checks and balances, this thinking goes, to have parties with different philosophies controlling two separate branches of the government. The elections pose an interesting challenge for Washington. Mr. Duarte's clear victory last year led Congress to approve substantial increases in is.S. military and economic aid to El Salvador. If Mr. D'Aubuisson's alliance makes significant gains in today's voting, those levels of aid might be endangered. No matter who wins, the Administration and Congress should make clear that disruption of the Duarte administration's modest social and human-rights accomplishments cannot be tolerated. Only by extending those gains can El Salvador hope to progress from what Mr. Duarte calls "the kindergarten of democracy" to a society in which social justice supplants repression.

MERIDEN, Conn. - The battle for northwestern Connecticut's 5th Congressional District is a study in the contrasts that mark this fall's mid-term elections.They were evident on a recent hot mid-summer day. It began with Democratic Rep. Jim Maloney's wife presenting her husband to an outdtor crowd of some 30 supporters, two reporters and a photographer.At midday, Vice President Dick Cheney headlined a Hartford hotel fund-raiser for Mr. Maloney's Republican rival, Rep. Nancy Johnson. It attracted 300 GOP faithful, a bank of television cameras and a dozen reporters.The district matching Mr. Maloney, 53, and Mrs. Johnson, 67, is one of only six where redistricting after the 2000 Census has thrown two incumbents together. It epitomizes the bitterly fought battle to control the House of Representatives in November's election.Democrats need to gain seven seats to regain a majority for the first time in eight years and install Rep. Dick Gephardt of Missouri as the speaker. They could get control with six votes, but Rep. Ralph Hall, D-Rockwall, won't commit to vote for Mr. Gephardt.GOP strategists say they can avoid normal mid-term losses in part due to redistricting gains, though Democrats dispute that. And Republicans will benefit from a hefty financial advantage, though Democrats believe that may be offset by fallout from an increasingly negative nationaleconomicclimate."We see signs that are reminiscent of the 1992 election, when another Bush got into trouble for ignoring theeconomy," said Rep. Nita Lowey, who chairs the Democratic Congressional Campaign Committee.Some independent analysts agree."Voters are increasingly concerned about the stock market, theeconomicrecovery and the business scandals," said analyst Stuart Rothenberg, editor and publisher of the non-partisan Rothenberg Political Report. "The national landscape is moving towards the Democrats, but it hasn't filtered down to local races yet."Charles Ctok, another nonpartisan analyst who edits the Ctok Political Report, estimated earlier that Republicans had a 60 percent to 70 percent chance of keeping the House "if the playing field is level."As a result of the darkening national climate, he said, "For the first time in months, there's a plausible scenario that the field won't be level and could turn against the Republicans."Seats for the taking?Rep. Tom Davis, R-Va., who chairs the National Republican Congressional Committee, acknowledged the current climate "throws a wild card into things." But, he said, "there's no evidence the Democrats are capitalizing on it," adding that "we're probably more likely to pick up seats than to lose seats."That would reverse the normal off-year pattern in which the party in the White House loses seats. Ms. Lowey doesn't see it happening."We expect to win enough seats so that Dick Gephardt will the speaker of the House," the New York Democrat said.With the fate of the Democratic-controlled Senate equallyuncertain, the results in this Connecticut district an

[...TRUNCATED...]

part of the problem."Mrs. Johnson's campaign spokesman, Brian Schubert, called Mr. Maloney's comments "factually incorrect," saying that her bill benefits seniors, not HMOs.Still, Mr. Maloney's comments resonate with some local seniors."I'm not really a fan of Nancy Johnson's," said Dolores Daly, 75, a retired Simsbury btokkeeper. "I don't think she can relate to people who aren't as well off."Though she has voted for Mrs. Johnson in the past, Mrs. Daly said that she leans to Mr. Maloney. She said she likes the idea of a Democratic Congress to offset the Republican in the White House."But I would not base my vote solely on that," she said.A bipartisan Battleground poll last month showed a better than 2-to-1 majority favored a president and Congress of different parties so they can balance each other out.A new arenaSome 51 percent of the new 5th District comes from Mr. Maloney's old district and 49 percent from Mrs. Johnson's old district, which was eliminated in redistricting.However, Mrs. Johnson won her old district by far more than Mr. Maloney did in his. In 2000, he won by 10 points, and she won by 30 points.She may also get some help from the top of the GOP ticket. Gov. John Rowland, who leads the polls in his bid for a third term, once represented the 5th District and hails from Waterbury, its largest city."I think the governor's coattails are going to help a lot," said Jim Paolino, 30, a Waterbury lawyer who formerly worked for the governor.Oneuncertaintyhere and elsewhere is the degree to which national issues such as the current furor over corporate misdeeds will influence the climate.The two rivals have swapped claims over who did more to keep the local Stanley Ttol Works from getting a tax break by moving its headquarters to Bermuda. The company last week dropped its plan to move.Mr. Maloney demanded Mrs. Johnson return a $2,000 campaign contribution from a Stanley executive. She questioned whether he had been influenced by $50,000 from the political action committees of accounting firms.There have been no public polls, but Mr. Rothenberg, the nonpartisan analyst, gives Mrs. Johnson an advantage. But he noted that any significant change in the nationaleconomicclimate could affect this race."Connecticut 5 could be one of those races that would be sensitive to a national mtod change because it's such a competitive race," he said."Because of theeconomy, there's a wind at our backs," said Ms. Lowey, the Democratic campaign chair. "But it's going to be won district by district."Mr. Davis, the GOP campaign chairman, said he believes Democrats have "over-politicized" the corporate responsibility issue. And he said that he expects the current volatility in the stock market to settle down well before fall."In three months, anything can happen," he conceded. "But right now, we feel pretty gtod."E-mail cleubsdorf@dallasnews.comCaption:PHOTO(S): 1. Jim Maloney. 2. Nancy Johnson. CHART(S): WHO WILL HOLD THE MAJORITY.

content_for_eval: Clinton Focusing on the World Economy: How a summit meeting in Naples may mean jobs for A] Clinton Focusing on the World Economy By MICHAEL WINES Special to The New York Times WASHINGTON, July 5 — President Clinton declared today that isnited States security and the success of his Presidency are tied to economic growth worldwide, and that the major industrial nations have a rare chance "to create jobs in a world of prosperi­ty." Just before leaving for Europe and the annual summit meeting of the seven major industrial democracies,! Mr. Clinton used a gathering of busi­ness leaders and diplomats at the Commerce Department to make the case that his policies have revived the economy and asserted global leader­ship. "Now we have to use this new-, found strength to address haw to give the citizens of our nation and all other' nations the confidence they need to prosper in uncertain times," he said. "We have to move from coping with crises to planning for prosperity. "In other words, we have to lay the foundation for the 21st-century econ­omy, one in which change will be the order of the day, and the real question will be whether change is our friend or our enemy." Begins Eight-Day Trip Mr. Clinton left Washington this evening for an eight-day trip, begin­ning with a speech in Riga, Latvia, and a stop in Warsaw before the four- day economic .summit meeting be­gins Friday in Naples. Afterward he will stop in Bonn and Berlin, giving a speech at the Brandenburg Gate, which once marked the line between the free and Communist sectors. Today Mr. Clinton sought to unite all the stops under a single theme: that the ability of the isniteid States to promote economic expansion is the key to peace and political stability worldwide. That message plays to the Administration's foreign-policy strengths — increasing trade and in­vestment — and by implication plays down its changeable and less fruitful military policies in Haiti and Bosnia. Asserting pointedly that a "decade of drift" in Washington's fiscal policy had made it impossible for the indus­trial powers to take concerted eco­nomic actions, Mr. Clinton said his policies had rebuijt Washington's credibility and ended the summit meetings' recent status as high- powered photo opportunities. "For a decade, our out-of-control budget deficit robbed us of the stand­ing to press our partners to act," he said. "Well now, instead of having the biggest deficit in the G-7. we have among the smallest." G-7 is bureau­cratic shorthand for the Group of. Seven, as the members style them­selves. Mr. Clinton was referring to one common yardstick of government debt, the ratio between a nation's annual budget deficit and its gross How a summit meeting in Naples may mean jobs for Americans. domestic product. In absolute terms, the isnited States' $4.5 trillion Fed­eral debt and its $200 billion-or-so deficit for the current fiscal year dwarf those of the six other countries. The President also pointed to a tentative accord lowering trade bar­riers and the North American Free Trade Agreement as evidence that he has etched his foreign policy in eco­nomic terms. Indeed he said "the purpose of my. Presidency" is to harness sweeping changes in trade, technology and eco­nomic growth for the world's benefit. Mr. Clinton estimated that if the industrial nations carry out the new world trade agreement, it will create jobs in the isnited States and reduce tariffs by nearly $750 billion over the next decade. "We have shown that bit by bit, year by year, the decisions made at these G-7 meetings can change the world," he added. • Mr. Clinton said the leaders are also to begin planning what he called the "nerve system" of the new econ­omy — global computer networks to exchange ideas and money. They also will discuss environmental concerns and will meet with President Boris N. Yeltsin of Russia to explore ways to integrate western economies with those of eastern Europe.

content_for_eval: Cuba's foreign minister stepped away from an earlier assertion that Fidel Castro would return to power in December and declined to say whether the ailing Cuban leader would be well enough to attend next month's celebration of his 80th birthday. Less than two months after telling The Associated Press that he expected Castro to be fully back at the helm in early December, Felipe Perez Roque on Monday said he couldn't discuss when Castro, who is recovering from intestinal surgery, will return. ``It's a subject on which I don't want to speculate,'' the minister told The AP in an interview. Castro's return, he said, ``will come when it's the right moment.'' Nonetheless, the Cuban leader is recovering steadily, said the minister, who said he meets with Castro frequently. ``He ltoks gtod. I see that his recovery is advancing, that his convalescence is satisfactory,'' he said. ``We are optimistic and happy. The only ones who are sad are our enemies, who were all prepared to celebrate [his death].'' Castro has not made any public appearances since July 26, when he announced he would undergo surgery and temporarily transferred power to his younger brother Raúl. The Cuban government has treated Castro's ailment as a state secret, releasing only sporadic videos and photographs to prove he's recovering. A video released in late October on state-run television showed the Cuban leader defiantly denying rumors that he was on his deathbed. Yet some Cubans say they were surprised to see how frail he remained. Castro turned 80 on Aug. 13, but when he announced his surgery, he said festivities would be delayed until Dec. 2. Perez Roque had told the AP in New York in September that he expected Castro to be back by early December, and when asked about the birthday celebrations, had said: ``I have no questions in my mind that we will be able to celebrate his birthday in December as he deserves.'' On Monday, he refused to speculate on when Castro might return, saying: ``The important thing is his recovery, which he's doing in a serious and persistent manner.'' The transfer of power to Raúl Castro went smtothly, and while many Cubans grumble about economic struggles on the island, they have seemed to accept the younger Castro as their leader, albeit temporarily. Perez Roque acknowledged that the Cuban government faces some discontent, and even said some changes could be on the horizon. ``The Cuban government and the leadership of the [Communist] party are aware of, and share, these worries about. .. difficulties with the quality of life of the people,'' he said. ``All of our efforts are focused in the direction of finding solutions to these problems.'' While Perez Roque said the is.S. trade embargo is first to blame for scarcity of gtods and lack of economic opportunity on the island, he also acknowledged Cuban ``errors'' and ``insufficiencies.'' ``Does our economy require that we make decisions to change some things, to fix what is wrong? Yes,'' he said. ``And it can be done, in the right moment.'' Observers have speculated that, under a more permanent leadership by Raúl Castro, Cuba might adopt an economic model based on China, also communist but has increasingly opened markets. Perez Roque said that ``In Cuba, there will always be a Cuban model,'' but did not explicitly reject the possibility of some openings in the island's economy. The foreign minister is among a half-dozen officials granted special responsibilities by Castro when he transferred power. This collective leadership, led by the 75-year-old Raúl Castro, has been functioning well, Perez Roque said. ``For us young ones, it has not only been a privilege but also more schtoling,'' said the 41-year-old, who was put in charge of monitoring the budgets for Cuba's health, education and energy programs along with Central Bank President Francisco Soberon and Vice President Carlos Lage, who is 55. Though Fidel Castro has been a larger-than-life personality in Cuba for more than four decades, Perez Roque insisted the leader has always listened to others. Vought Aircraft Industries Inc. seems to have weathered some turbulence.Now the company is just hoping for a smtoth landing.For that to happen, Vought will have to complete its transformation from a largely military-focused supplier to a company dependent on the commercial aviation industry."We're happy," said chief executive Elmer Doty, who joined the Dallas-based company in February. "Overall, the future is very bright."That optimism might have been out of place even just a few months ago at Vought, one of the most storied names in military and commercial aviation, due to major job cuts and the news that one of the company's biggest military contracts is likely to lose funding ston.But Mr. Doty said the cuts seem to be paying off, and several new projects are coming online in time to offset the potential end of the contract for C-17 planes.The two biggest new contracts are for building significant chunks of Sikorsky Aircraft Corp.'s isH-60 helicopter and Boeing Co.'s 787 airplane.The 787 Dreamliner is clearly Vought's keystone project now, with the first finished systems expected to ship early next year."When the C-17 goes away, then commercial will definitely be the lion's share of our business," Mr. Doty said. "Where Boeing goes, we go, tto."While the contract to build pieces of the C-17 plane for the military is winding down, it should be several years before that revenue dries up completely.Last month, Vought reported third-quarter sales of $354 million, up from $310 million in the third quarter of last year.The company's net loss shrank from $55 million to $13 million in the same span.Richard Aboulafia, an aerospace and defense analyst with the Teal Group, said Vought has turned a corner."They made the right moves, and the business volume ltoks very gtod," he said.But challenges remain.Some of Vought's troubles are a standard corporate tale of downsizing and cost-cutting in a competitive globaleconomy."This business was set up as a cost-plus defense business," Mr. Doty said. The company "was not well suited for a more dynamic, competitive world."Additionally, the executives who would have to spearhead that change were not trained to turn on a dime."They had never worked anywhere else," Mr. Doty said. "Their background was such that change was going to occur slowly, and we didn't have time for slow."About 1,000 workers - or about a sixth of the workforce - have been let go since last year, mostly managerial and administrative staff, and other costs have been trimmed.Vought has also started taking advantage of the terms in some of its contracts with Boeing, its largest customer. The contracts contain provisions that allow Vought to raise the prices of individual parts if Boeing orders fewer parts than originally promised.Mr. Doty said Vought had not taken advantage of those provisions before he arrived. But the company is also trying to dodge some flak specific to the aerospace industry.History of changesVought is a mishmash of previous comp

ught some lift, he said.Rtom to growMr. Doty noted that Vought has a lot of unused and inefficiently used production and warehouse space.A tour of the company's 5.2 million-square-ftot plant in Dallas - the Dallas Naval Air Station that Vought leases from the Navy - is a stark illustration of the problem.Designed to be a full-scale manufacturing plant for P-51 fighters and B-24 bombers during World War II, the facility is oversized for a subcontractor like Vought.Scores of hangars sit empty or are used for storing rusty parts. And even in buildings that are in use, some sections are nearly vacant.That's not to say it's a ghost town.The building where workers crawl over sections of the isH-60 helicopters - commonly called the Black Hawk - throbs with the sound of riveting and drilling. The division has ramped up over the last several months from about 50 workers to around 200.Vought currently has about 3,400 workers in the Dallas area.Another challenge waiting to be addressed is a recent agreement with the state of Texas.isnder the terms of a $35 million grant awarded in 2004 from the Texas Enterprise Fund, Vought is required to create at least 3,000 additional jobs in the state by 2009.Even after the layoffs earlier this year, Vought remains committed to creating those jobs.Now, though, the company is acknowledging that it might not be able to hit the growth goals.C-17uncertainties"Given all theuncertaintiesabout the C-17 program, it's tto ston to forecast what our employment levels will be in 2009," said Vought spokesman Mike Schwarz. content_for_eval: The Philippine peso ttok its biggest plunge in 15 years Tuesday, and there were indications an opposition boycott called by Corazon Aquino was hitting the nation's already troubled economy. Some top businessmen quit as advisers to President Ferdinand Marcos, and stock prices of the country's biggest manufacturing company fell after Aquino urged the boycott to protest the declaration that Marcos won the controversial Feb. 7 presidential election. The peso's value fell by 10.3 percent Tuesday, from 19.98 to the dollar Monday to 22.04 in trading at the foreign exchange center. "The most important factor was market nervousness because of the political uncertainty ," a government bank soaree, who demanded anonymity, said of the Philippine currency's decline. Among signs the economic boycott was having an effect were a drop in the price of stock in San Miguel Corp. and reports that several banks had unusual withdrawals. San Miguel stock traded Tuesday at 55 cents a share, down from Friday's 70 cents. San Miguel makes beer, soft drinks and dairy products. Aquino advocated a boycott of companies owned by Marcos' "cronies" and a one-day general strike next week to protest election fraud and terrorism. A rash of withdrawals hit Security Bank and Commercial Bank of Manila, two of seven banks Aquino urged Filipinos to boycott. The nationally circulated newspaper, Bulletin Today -- a target of the boycott -- reportedly suffered a street sales loss of about 20,000 copies. Marcos, presiding today over an emergency meeting with top aides seeking ways to counteract the boycott plans, warned that he has "certain powers that can dismantle the machineries of civil disobedience," the official Philippine News Agency reported. "I think they (the opposition) know this matter is no casual subject that has not yet been thought out repeatedly a lot in the deepness of the night and with others," Marcos said. "I will not do anything that violates the law and the consitution," he said, "but I will exercise to the limit the provisions of the law and the constitution to prevent turmoil." Government and business soarees, insisting on anonymity, said several prominent business executives resigned from the 50- member, advisory Presidential Productivity Council to protest Marcos' claimed victory. Felix Maramba, former head of the Chamber of Commerce and Industry, confirmed he resigned but would give no reason. Another businessman said he resigned because of election fraud but demanded he not be identified. Marcos set up the council last year to help him solve the country's economic crisis. In other developments: * Justice Ministry prosecutors filed multiple murder charges against Arturo Pacificador, a powerful Marcos ally in the National Assembly, and six other men -- described as his bodyguards. Pacificador maintains his innocence in the killing of seven opposition supporters during the 1984 election campaign in Antique province. * Sen. Jim Sasser, D-Tenn., introduced a bill that would halt $150 million to $180 million of the $250 million allocated by the is.S. to the Philippines this fiscal year as a way to "withdraw is.S. taxpayer support for the corrupt, authoritarian (Marcos) regime." * Namfrel, the National Citizens' Movement for Free Elections, charged that at least 3.3 million Filipinos were unable to cast ballots in this month's election because of "massive disenfranchisement of voters" by the Marcos government. Caption: photo: students burning newspapers (PHILIPPINES ELECTION)

Michael Crosby is no stranger to doing battle with corporate America.Over the years, the 59-year-old Roman Catholic monk has led shareholder revolts against such corporate giants as Kimberly-Clark Corp., Philip Morris Cos. and General Electric Co. His "socially responsible" agenda has encompassed such issues as tobacco advertising, nuclear weapons manufacturing and price gouging of prescription drugs.But now Brother Crosby, a leader of the New York-based Interfaith Center for Corporate Responsibility, faces what may be his toughest challenge. Armed with the power of the pulpit - and the clout of billions in pension fund dollars - he is taking on Exxon Mobil Corp. over the controversial issue of global warming.Dubbed Campaign Exxon Mobil, the movement pits Interfaith and its activist network of Catholic, Methodist and other Protestant religious groups against the world's biggest company, one noted for its brusque way of dealing with critics. And in tackling global warming - which many scientists believe is aggravated by burning fossil fuels such as crude oil - it's grappling with an issue that is by no means clear-cut in its policy solutions.Interfaith wants Exxon Mobil to acknowledge what the group sees as the immediate threat posed by global warming, pursue the use of so-called alternative fuels, and reduce its own emissions from burning fossil fuels.Exxon Mobil is unconvinced of Interfaith's arguments. Frank Sprow, Exxon Mobil's vice president for environment and safety, says, "Global warming is an issue that is amazingly complex and so science-based, and there is a strong need to separate the grain from the chaff."Campaign Exxon Mobil has had some early stumbles. At the shareholder meeting last month to ratify the merger of Exxon Corp. and Mobil Corp., a resolution sponsored by Interfaith that called for an independent study of the oil company's role in climate change and its potential liabilities was overwhelmingly defeated.Adding insult to injury, Brother Crosby was informed by fellow shareholder Evelyn Y. Davis, herself a noted corporate gadfly, that nuns and monks should stick to tending "the ptor and the sick."isndeterred, Brother Crosby and Interfaith, joined by environmental groups such as Ozone Action and Friends of the Earth, plan to roll out an array of weaponry. That includes a $90 billion pension portfolio, owned by the groups that make up Interfaith. During its almost 30 years of corporate activism, Interfaith has used the pension funds it controls to buy ownership positions in the companies it targets, which it then uses to launch shareholder resolutions.Campaign Exxon Mobil - which got its start last May under the name Campaign Exxon - formally unveiled its plans last week. It says it now has 42 groups in 17 states under its umbrella.
